You’re not asserting a real person who does the action. - No hay nadie que lo sepa. - No existe nada que pueda hacerlo. - No conozco a nadie que viva allí. (Here you’re not saying “I know someone”; you’re saying you don’t. The existence/identification is denied → SUBJ.) Compare: - Conozco a alguien que vive allí. (There is someone; identified → IND.)
What about “el hecho de que”? I thought that’s factual, but it often takes subjunctive too.

“El hecho de que” is interesting because it can introduce either: - a fact you’re presenting as established → indicative can appear - content you’re evaluating or using as a premise in an emotional/judgment frame → subjunctive often appears Examples: - El hecho de que llegó tarde causó problemas. (Here it’s treated as a fact; IND “llegó.”) - Me molesta el hecho de que llegue tarde. (Reaction/judgment; SUBJ “llegue.”) - Es sorprendente el hecho de que haya ganado. (Reaction; SUBJ) So the surrounding structure matters. If it’s embedded under an evaluative predicate, subjunctive is likely.
So basically everything depends on “speaker stance.” That’s annoying but I guess it’s the point.

Right. The subjunctive is less about the event and more about how the speaker frames it. That’s why a purely mechanical “if word X then subjunctive” rule always has exceptions. If you want something more mechanical, focus on the highest-frequency fixed structures where the choice is stable: - Ojalá + SUBJ - Para que + SUBJ - Antes de que + SUBJ - Es importante/necesario que + SUBJ - Querer/pedir/recomendar que + SUBJ - No creer/no pensar que + SUBJ - Es cierto/obvio que + IND These cover a lot of real-life sentences.
Let’s drill more. But make them the kind of sentences I actually try to say, not weird textbook ones.

Choose IND or SUBJ and (if you can) conjugate the verb in parentheses. I’ll correct. 1) No pienso que (ser) buena idea. 2) Me da miedo que (pasar) algo. 3) Es verdad que (tener) razón. 4) No es verdad que (tener) razón. 5) Busco un sitio donde (poder) estudiar tranquilo. 6) Tengo un sitio donde (poder) estudiar tranquilo. 7) Cuando (terminar) esto, me voy. (meaning: as soon as I finish—future) 8) Cuando (terminar) esto, me iba. (meaning: whenever I finished—habitual in the past)
1 sea. 2 pase. 3 tienes. 4 tengas. 5 pueda. 6 puedo. 7 termine. 8 terminaba.

Nice—almost perfect. 1) “No pienso que sea buena idea.” Correct. 2) “Me da miedo que pase algo.” Correct. 3) “Es verdad que tienes razón.” Correct. 4) “No es verdad que tengas razón.” Correct. 5) “Busco un sitio donde pueda estudiar tranquilo.” Correct (non-specific place). 6) “Tengo un sitio donde puedo estudiar tranquilo.” Correct (specific place you have). 7) “Cuando termine esto, me voy.” Correct (future). 8) “Cuando terminaba esto, me iba.” Grammatically fine, but more natural would be “Cuando terminaba (de hacer) esto, me iba” or “Cuando terminaba esto, me iba a casa,” depending on context. Your mood choice (IND) is correct because it’s habitual in the past.
Okay. Another: “No creo que es…” I keep accidentally doing that. Is there any trick to stop?

Two tricks: 1) Chunk it: memorize “No creo que…” as a single unit that automatically demands subjunctive. Like a reflex: “No creo que + SUBJ.” Don’t allow yourself to “complete” it with indicative. 2) Use a substitute verb that forces you into the right pattern while speaking: instead of “No creo que…,” try “Dudo que…” (also subjunctive). If you can say “Dudo que sea…” you’ll avoid “No creo que es…” Also, practice with minimal pairs aloud: - Creo que es… - No creo que sea… Repeat until your mouth expects “sea” after “no creo que.”
The worst is “creo que no…” Like “Creo que no viene” is indicative, right? But it has “no” and then I panic.

Yes: “Creo que no viene” uses indicative because the main clause “creo que” is affirmative belief; you’re asserting your belief about a negative proposition (“he’s not coming”). The negation is inside the subordinate clause, not negating the belief. Compare: - Creo que no viene. (I believe he isn’t coming.) → IND - No creo que venga. (I don’t believe he’s coming.) → SUBJ So the question is: what is negated—your belief (“no creo”) or the content (“no viene”)?
Okay that’s actually clear. Negation scope. I can work with that.
Exactly. Scope is a concrete way to think about it.
Another confusing area: “porque.” Do you ever use subjunctive after “porque”?

Yes, but not in the straightforward “because” sense. Indicative is normal after “porque” when you’re giving a real cause: - Lo hice porque era necesario. (real reason) Subjunctive can appear after “porque” when the cause is negated or questioned—when it’s not presented as an established reason: - No lo hice porque fuera fácil. (I didn’t do it because it was easy—implies that wasn’t the reason; maybe I did it for another reason.) - Lo hice no porque me gustara, sino porque era necesario. (not because I liked it—SUBJ— but because it was necessary—IND) So: “porque” usually IND; SUBJ shows “alleged/denied reason.”
That “no… porque…” structure is so subtle. I’d never produce that.
You don’t need to produce it immediately; just recognize it so it doesn’t throw you. For production, focus on the high-frequency triggers first.
Speaking of high frequency: “para que” always subjunctive? Even if it’s like a general purpose?

“Para que + SUBJ” is extremely consistent because it expresses purpose with an intended outcome, not a fact. - Te lo digo para que lo sepas. - Cerré la puerta para que no entrara el ruido. - Estudia para que apruebes. If there’s no subject change and you can use an infinitive, Spanish often prefers “para + infinitivo”: - Estudio para aprobar. (same subject) - Estudio para que mis padres estén tranquilos. (different subject → subjunctive)
So subject change matters there.
Exactly. “Para + infinitive” when the subject is the same; “para que + subjunctive” when it’s different (or when you want to emphasize the goal in a clause).
What about “sin que”? That one also subjunctive?

Yes, “sin que + SUBJ” is standard: - Se fue sin que nadie lo viera. - Lo resolvió sin que yo lo ayudara. Again, if there’s no subject change, you can often use “sin + infinitivo”: - Se fue sin decir nada. (same subject)
Okay. I want to tackle “como” now. “Como si” uses subjunctive, right? But why?

“Como si” introduces an unreal or hypothetical comparison (“as if”), so Spanish uses imperfect subjunctive (or pluperfect subjunctive for earlier time). - Habla como si supiera todo. (as if he knew everything—imperfect subjunctive) - Me miró como si hubiera visto un fantasma. (as if he had seen a ghost—pluperfect subjunctive) It’s not about time, it’s about unreality: the comparison is not asserted as true.
And “como” by itself?

“Como” can be many things: 1) “Como” = “as” (manner): usually indicative/normal clause - Hazlo como te dije. (how I told you—IND because it’s a fact that I told you) 2) “Como” in threats/conditions (“If you…” kind of): - Como llegues tarde, nos vamos. (If you show up late, we’re leaving.) This uses present subjunctive in many varieties of Spanish (especially Spain), functioning like a conditional warning. 3) “Como” in “as soon as/when” is usually “como” + subjunctive in some set phrases, but less common than “cuando.” So “como si” is the stable one: always subjunctive.
I’m also confused about “lo que” and “quien” in relative clauses. Like “Lo que sea” vs “lo que es.”

With “lo que” and “quien,” mood choice again tracks whether it’s specific/known vs indefinite/hypothetical. - Haré lo que pueda. (whatever I can—indefinite; SUBJ) - Haré lo que puedo. (what I can—more factual, implying known limits; IND) - Quien lo sepa, que lo diga. (whoever knows it—indefinite; SUBJ) - El que lo sabe, lo dice. (the one who knows it—specific class; often IND) Fixed-ish expressions: - Sea lo que sea… (whatever it may be) - Pase lo que pase… (whatever happens) These use subjunctive as a kind of “free-choice / regardless” structure.
“Pase lo que pase” is subjunctive because… it’s unknown?
Unknown and also “regardless of outcome.” It’s not asserting any particular event; it’s opening a set of possible events and saying the main clause holds in all cases.
I think part of my problem is I learned subjunctive as “doubt” only. But it’s way more.

Exactly. “Doubt” is just one subset. If you reframe it as “not presented as a settled fact,” it unifies: - doubt (not settled) - desire (not reality yet) - emotion/judgment (not asserting, reacting) - hypothetical conditions (not settled) - non-specific referents (not identified) - future time clauses (not settled)
Let’s do more rapid-fire but include “aunque,” “cuando,” and relative clauses. I want to stop hesitating.

Choose IND or SUBJ and conjugate. 1) Aunque (ser) caro, lo compro. (meaning: it is expensive, but I’ll buy it) 2) Aunque (ser) caro, lo compraré. (meaning: even if it’s expensive, I’ll buy it) 3) Te llamaré en cuanto (llegar). 4) Siempre te llamo en cuanto (llegar) a casa. 5) No hay nada que (poder) hacer. 6) Hay algo que (poder) hacer. 7) Busco el informe que (estar) en la mesa. (specific) 8) Busco un informe que (estar) en la mesa. (non-specific; any report that is on the table)
1 es. 2 sea. 3 llegue. 4 llego. 5 pueda. 6 puedo. 7 está. 8 esté.
All correct. That’s exactly the pattern you want to internalize.
Why is #6 indicative? “Hay algo que puedo hacer.” Couldn’t it be “pueda”?

It can be either, with a meaning shift. - Hay algo que puedo hacer. = There is something I can do. You’re asserting that there exists a specific actionable thing within your ability (more “real/identified”). - Hay algo que pueda hacer. = Is there something I could do? / There is something that I might be able to do (often used in questions, polite offers, or when the “something” is not identified and you’re focusing on possibility). In your sentence as stated (affirmative assertion), indicative is the default.
That’s… subtle. But at least it’s consistent with “specific vs unknown.”
Exactly: indicative tends to “pin it down.”
Another issue: I overuse subjunctive because I’m scared. Like I’ll say “Sé que sea difícil” which is wrong, right?

Right. “Sé que…” asserts knowledge, so it takes indicative: - Sé que es difícil. Subjunctive after “saber que” would be very unusual and typically wrong. A helpful list of “indicative anchors”: - sé que - es cierto que - es verdad que - es obvio/evidente que - está claro que - resulta que - veo que - oigo que - noto que These are fact/perception/reporting frames → indicative.
“Resulta que” is indicative even if it’s surprising?

Yes, because it still introduces an asserted fact (“it turns out that…”): - Resulta que no tenía razón. - Resulta que era mentira. If you say “Me sorprende que…” then you’re reacting → subjunctive: - Me sorprende que no tenga razón.
Okay. What about “es raro que” vs “es raro” without que?

“Es raro que + SUBJ” is a judgment about a proposition: - Es raro que no me llame. If you’re describing a noun: - Es raro el ruido. (rare/strange noise) No subjunctive issue because no subordinate clause. If you use an infinitive: - Es raro no verme en el espejo. (rare not to see myself) Again no subjunctive. So “es raro que” is one of those evaluative triggers.
And “puede ser que” is subjunctive, right?
Yes: - Puede ser que venga. - Puede ser que hayan salido ya.
But “puede que” also subjunctive. Same thing.
Exactly.
I want you to correct a bunch of sentences I’ve actually written. I’ll paste them and you tell me what’s wrong and why, but don’t just say “subjunctive because X,” I want the meaning.
Paste them. I’ll correct each, label IND/SUBJ, and explain the stance/meaning difference.

1) No pienso que es necesario. 2) Me alegro que vienes. 3) Busco un libro que es corto. 4) Cuando llego mañana, te llamo. 5) Aunque llueve mañana, salgo. 6) Espero que lo haces hoy. 7) No es posible que lo hace. 8) Creo que no venga.

1) No pienso que sea necesario. Meaning: You’re not asserting necessity; you’re rejecting the idea. “No pienso que…” → non-commitment → SUBJ. 2) Me alegro de que vengas / Me alegro de que hayas venido (depending on time). Meaning: You’re expressing reaction, not stating a fact. Emotion → SUBJ. Also “alegrarse de que” normally uses “de.” 3) Busco un libro que sea corto. Meaning: You want any book with that property; non-specific antecedent → SUBJ. If you mean a particular book you already know exists, then “que es corto” could work, but your sentence reads like “any book.” 4) Cuando llegue mañana, te llamo. Meaning: “tomorrow when I arrive” is future, not yet realized → SUBJ. (If habitual: “Cuando llego, te llamo” = whenever I arrive, I call you.) 5) Aunque llueva mañana, salgo. Meaning: Tomorrow’s rain is uncertain; “even if it rains” → SUBJ. If it’s already raining now: “Aunque llueve, salgo.” 6) Espero que lo hagas hoy. Meaning: “Espero que…” expresses desire/hope, not a factual report → SUBJ. 7) No es posible que lo haga. Meaning: You’re evaluating the proposition as impossible, not asserting it occurs → SUBJ. 8) Creo que no viene. Meaning: You’re asserting your belief about a negative proposition → IND. If you mean “I don’t believe he’s coming,” then: “No creo que venga.”
This is exactly my mess. Especially #8. I always invert it.

Practice with these two as a paired drill: - Creo que no viene. (I believe he’s not coming.) - No creo que venga. (I don’t believe he’s coming.) Say them out loud back-to-back until they feel like two separate patterns.

Another thing: “If” clauses. I know “Si tengo tiempo, voy” uses indicative. But then I see “Si tuviera tiempo, iría” with imperfect subjunctive. That’s conditional. Is that the only time “si” uses subjunctive?

Mostly, yes. With “si”: - Real/possible conditions → indicative: - Si tengo tiempo, voy. - Si tenía tiempo, iba. (habitual past) - Si tuve tiempo, fui. (specific past) - Hypothetical/unreal conditions → imperfect subjunctive: - Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (If I had time, I would go.) - Si fuera rico, compraría una casa. - Past unreal (counterfactual) → pluperfect subjunctive: - Si hubiera tenido tiempo, habría ido. (If I had had time, I would have gone.) One strong “don’t do this” rule: “Si” does not take the conditional tense in the “if” clause in standard Spanish: - Incorrect: Si tendría tiempo, iría. - Correct: Si tuviera tiempo, iría.
Okay that’s one clear rule at least.
Yes—hang onto that one.
I also struggle with “como si” vs “si.” Like “Si fuera…” and “Como si fuera…” both use subjunctive but different meaning.
Right: - “Si fuera…” sets a condition: If I were… - “Como si fuera…” sets a comparison: as if I were… Both are unreal/hypothetical, hence subjunctive, but the logic differs: condition vs comparison.
Can you give me a compact “decision tree” I can run mentally? Like, I see a “que” clause and I decide mood.

A mental decision tree for “que + clause”: 1) Is it reporting a fact/perception/knowledge? - “Sé/veo/está claro/es cierto/resulta que…” → IND If yes, stop. 2) Is it a request, desire, influence, or recommendation? - “Quiero/pido/recomiendo/insisto en que…” → SUBJ If yes, stop. 3) Is it emotion/judgment/importance? - “Me alegra/molesta/es importante/es una pena/es raro que…” → SUBJ If yes, stop. 4) Is it doubt/denial/possibility? - “Dudo/no creo/no es seguro/es posible…” → SUBJ If yes, stop. 5) Is it an expression like “no dudo que / no cabe duda de que” that actually asserts certainty? - “No dudo que / no hay duda de que” → IND If yes, stop. If none of these, check whether it’s a relative clause (a “que” meaning “that/which/who” after a noun). If so, decide: specific/known → IND; non-specific/desired/negated existence → SUBJ.
That’s helpful. But I still get tripped by “insistir en que.” That’s subjunctive? Even though it’s kind of assertive?

“Insistir en que” usually takes subjunctive because it’s an influence/pressure verb—pushing someone to do something or accept a proposal. - Insisto en que vengas. (I insist that you come.) - Insistió en que lo llamáramos. However, “insistir en que” can also mean “to maintain/claim that” (asserting a statement). In that sense, indicative can appear: - Insiste en que tiene razón. (He insists/maintains that he’s right.) So again: two meanings: - insist = demand → SUBJ - insist = claim/maintain → IND Context usually makes it clear.
So many verbs have two modes. “Decir,” “insistir”… great.
True, but the ambiguity is limited to a handful of verbs. Most triggers are consistent.
I want to talk about “de que” vs “que.” Like “Me alegro de que…” you mentioned. Why “de”? And does it affect mood?

It’s just the preposition required by the verb/adjective, not the mood. “Alegrarse de” takes “de,” so when followed by a clause, you keep it: “Me alegro de que…” Other common ones: - Asegurarse de que… (make sure that…) often IND or SUBJ depending on meaning, but frequently SUBJ when it’s an objective to verify: “Asegúrate de que esté bien hecho.” - Estar seguro de que… typically IND because it’s certainty: “Estoy seguro de que viene.” - Darse cuenta de que… IND (realization): “Me di cuenta de que era tarde.” - Quejarse de que… SUBJ often (complaint/reaction): “Se queja de que no le hagan caso.” So “de” doesn’t trigger mood; the predicate does.
“Asegurarse de que” is confusing. Sometimes I see “Asegúrate de que está…” and sometimes “esté…”

Because it can be: 1) Verification of a fact (you want to confirm something is true) → indicative is possible: - Asegúrate de que está cerrado. (Make sure it is closed—treating “it is closed” as a state you check.) 2) Ensuring an outcome (you want to make it become true) → subjunctive is common: - Asegúrate de que esté cerrado. (Make sure it’s closed—more “ensure that it’s closed,” not just “verify.”) In practice, many speakers use subjunctive as the default with “asegúrate de que” because it often functions like an instruction to ensure. But indicative is not crazy when it’s clearly about checking an existing fact.
So it’s like “make sure that…” in English is also ambiguous.
Exactly.
Another phrase: “Lo que pasa es que…” always indicative?

Usually yes, because it introduces an explanation asserted as fact: - Lo que pasa es que no tengo tiempo. - Lo que pasa es que estaba cansado. If you embed it under something that changes stance (rare), you might see other moods, but as a practical rule: “Lo que pasa es que + IND.”
Okay. Can we do a longer exercise where you give a paragraph and I have to choose moods? I need something more realistic than isolated sentences.

Fill in the verbs with the right mood/tense. I’ll provide the base verb in parentheses. “Me molesta que siempre me (interrumpir) ____ cuando (intentar) ____ explicarte algo. No creo que (entender) ____ lo que (querer) ____ decir. Es posible que te (parecer) ____ una tontería, pero para mí es importante que me (escuchar) ____. Aunque no (estar) ____ de acuerdo, prefiero que (respetar) ____ mi turno. Cuando (terminar) ____ de hablar, ya (poder) ____ contestar.” Try to keep the meaning: first “cuando” is habitual; second “cuando” is future.
interrumpas, intento, entiendas, quiero, parezca, escuches, estés, respetes, termine, puedes.

Almost all correct; just one adjustment. - Me molesta que siempre me interrumpas. (SUBJ) Correct. - cuando intento explicarte algo. (IND habitual) Correct. - No creo que entiendas… (SUBJ) Correct. - lo que quiero decir. (IND because it’s “what I mean,” asserted) Correct. - Es posible que te parezca… (SUBJ) Correct. - es importante que me escuches. (SUBJ) Correct. - Aunque no estás de acuerdo… Here you chose “estés,” which would make it hypothetical (“even if you’re not in agreement”). But your sentence is “Aunque no ___ de acuerdo” in a context that sounds like you’re conceding a real situation (“even though you’re not in agreement”). Both are possible, but they mean different things: - Aunque no estás de acuerdo, prefiero que respetes mi turno. (You are not in agreement—known fact.) - Aunque no estés de acuerdo, prefiero que respetes mi turno. (Whether or not you’re in agreement.) Given the earlier “Es posible que…,” either could work. If the intended meaning is “even if you’re not,” then “estés” is correct. If it’s “even though you’re not,” then “estás.” I’ll accept your “estés” as consistent with a general principle. - prefiero que respetes… (SUBJ) Correct. - Cuando termine de hablar… (SUBJ future) Correct. - ya puedes contestar. (IND) Correct. So you’re making the right decisions; the only “soft spot” is choosing whether “aunque” is factual or hypothetical.
That was actually useful. But I hate that “aunque” can flip. I always choose wrong and then it changes my meaning.

If you want a safe strategy: when you’re not sure, decide whether you’re talking about a specific reality right now (IND) or a general concession (“even if”) (SUBJ). In real conversation, both are often acceptable; the listener rarely gets confused because context clarifies.
I also see subjunctive in main clauses, like “Viva el rey” or “Que tengas un buen día.” Is that the same subjunctive?

Yes, it’s the subjunctive used for: - Wishes: “Que tengas un buen día.” “Que te vaya bien.” “Que descanses.” - Exclamations/slogans: “¡Viva!” “¡Viva el rey!” - Soft commands in third person: “Que pase.” “Que lo haga.” It’s still the “non-assertion” idea: you’re not stating a fact; you’re expressing a wish or command.
Okay. Another nuance: “quizás” with indicative vs subjunctive. You said it depends on certainty. But how do I choose in practice?

In practice: - If you’re leaning toward it being true / you have evidence → indicative. - If you’re genuinely uncertain / speculating → subjunctive. Examples: - Quizás viene, porque me dijo que salía a las seis. (I have a reason → IND feels natural.) - Quizás venga; no tengo ni idea. (speculation → SUBJ.) Also, tense matters: - Quizás vino ayer. (IND past, fairly common) - Quizás haya venido ayer. (SUBJ perfect, more speculative) If you don’t want to overthink, default to subjunctive with “quizás/tal vez” when you’re guessing.
Sometimes I feel like I should just use indicative all the time and hope people get it.

People would still understand you, but you’d lose precision and you’d keep feeling that “something sounds off.” Since you’re benchmarking, it’s worth training the high-frequency patterns until they’re automatic. If you want a minimal “must-have” set: 1) querer/esperar/pedir/recomendar + que + SUBJ 2) no creer/no pensar/dudar + que + SUBJ 3) es importante/necesario/posible + que + SUBJ 4) es cierto/obvio + que + IND 5) when/as soon as/until in the future → SUBJ That alone will fix a lot.
I’m going to ask about “por más que.” Is that like “aunque”?

Yes, “por más que” and “por mucho que” often function like “no matter how much / even if,” and they commonly take subjunctive because they imply concession regardless of degree. - Por más que estudies, no lo vas a memorizar todo. (no matter how much you study) - Por mucho que lo intentes, no va a cambiar. Indicative can appear when the concession is treated as real and specific: - Por más que lo intentas, no lo consigues. (You keep trying; it’s a fact—more descriptive, less “regardless.”) But default: subjunctive.
What about “mientras”? I learned “mientras” can be “while” and “as long as.” Does that affect mood?

Yes. 1) “Mientras” = “while” describing a real simultaneous action → usually indicative: - Mientras cocino, escucho música. 2) “Mientras” = “as long as / provided that” introducing a condition → often subjunctive: - Puedes quedarte aquí mientras no hagas ruido. (as long as you don’t make noise) - Te apoyaré mientras digas la verdad. (as long as you tell the truth) Also, for future reference: - Te llamaré mientras esté allí. (while I’m there—future, not yet realized → SUBJ) So: factual ongoing/habitual → IND; conditional/future/unknown → SUBJ.
That last one is weird: “Te llamaré mientras esté allí.” It’s “while I’m there,” but it’s future so subjunctive.
Exactly. It’s the same principle as “cuando”: if it hasn’t happened yet, Spanish often uses subjunctive in these time clauses.
Okay. Let’s do another paragraph. Make it harder.

Fill in: “Quiero que me (decir) ____ la verdad, aunque te (parecer) ____ difícil. No es que no te (creer) ____, pero a veces dices cosas como si (saber) ____ más de lo que realmente (saber) ____. Si (tener) ____ pruebas, me las (mostrar) ____; y si no, prefiero que no (asegurar) ____ nada. Cuando (haber) ____ más información, lo (hablar) ____ con calma.”
digas, parezca, crea, supieras, sabes, tienes, muestras, asegures, haya, hablaré.

Good. Two refinements: - “Quiero que me digas la verdad” (SUBJ) correct. - “aunque te parezca difícil” (SUBJ, hypothetical concession) correct. - “No es que no te crea” (SUBJ) correct; this structure “no es que…” typically uses subjunctive because it frames a denial/clarification of motive. - “como si supieras” (imperfect subjunctive) correct. - “más de lo que realmente sabes” (IND) correct because it asserts what you really know (speaker treats it as fact). - “Si tienes pruebas, me las muestras” is grammatically okay (present indicative in both clauses) and sounds like a direct, forceful instruction (“If you have proof, you show it to me”). Many speakers might prefer: - Si tienes pruebas, muéstramelas. (imperative) - Si tienes pruebas, me las mostrarás. (strong expectation) But your form is acceptable. - “prefiero que no asegures nada” (SUBJ) correct. - “Cuando haya más información, lo hablaré con calma.” Here: “cuando” + future/unknown → subjunctive “haya” correct; main clause “hablaré” fine. So overall your mood choices are solid.
That “Si tienes pruebas, me las muestras” sounded off even to me. I was just copying the pattern.

Right—it’s not a mood problem; it’s pragmatic. Present indicative in the “then” clause can sound like an order or habitual. If you want neutral, use: - Si tienes pruebas, muéstramelas. (clear command) or - Si tienes pruebas, me las puedes mostrar. (polite) or - Si tienes pruebas, me las mostrarás. (firm expectation)
Another thing: “No es que…” always subjunctive? Like “No es que sea…”?

Typically yes: - No es que no quiera, es que no puedo. (It’s not that I don’t want to; it’s that I can’t.) - No es que sea difícil, pero lleva tiempo. (It’s not that it’s difficult, but it takes time.) It’s used to correct an inference or clarify a reason, and it frames the clause as something being denied as the explanation, hence subjunctive.
Okay. What about “el que” / “la que” in relative clauses? I get confused with “el que sea” etc.

Two main uses: 1) Relative pronoun “el que/la que/los que…” referring to a specific antecedent → usually indicative: - El que vive aquí es mi vecino. (the one who lives here—specific) 2) “The one that may be / whoever” as an indefinite choice → subjunctive: - El que sea, que lo diga. (whoever it is, let them say it) - La que venga primero, que espere. (whoever comes first, wait) Also fixed: - Sea quien sea… (whoever it may be) - Sea cual sea… (whatever it may be) Again: specific vs indefinite/free-choice.
I think the free-choice subjunctive is a whole category on its own.

It is, but it overlaps with “non-specific/hypothetical reference.” Spanish uses subjunctive to express “anyone/anything/wherever/whenever” in a generalizing way: - Donde estés, llámame. (wherever you are) - Hagas lo que hagas… (whatever you do) - Quienquiera que sea… (whoever it may be)
“Quienquiera que sea” is so formal.
Yes. More common is “quien sea” or “sea quien sea.”
I keep hearing that Spanish is “more subjunctive” than other languages. But then people say in some places it’s disappearing. Is that true?

Broadly: - Spanish uses subjunctive a lot compared to English because English often uses modal verbs (“might,” “should,” “want to”) or bare infinitives where Spanish uses a finite clause with subjunctive. - There is some variation and simplification in colloquial speech in some regions, but the subjunctive is not “disappearing” in any general sense. It remains a core part of standard Spanish and is very alive in everyday speech (especially in common frames like “quiero que,” “para que,” “ojalá,” “cuando llegue,” etc.). If you’re aiming for solid Spanish, learn it rather than relying on alleged erosion.
I’m still frustrated because it feels like I can understand explanations but I can’t produce it fast.

That’s normal; production lags behind comprehension. The fix is targeted repetition of the most common frames, not more theory. If you want a concrete routine: 1) Pick 10 triggers (quiero que, espero que, me alegra que, es importante que, no creo que, dudo que, es posible que, antes de que, para que, cuando + future). 2) For each, produce 5 sentences aloud daily. 3) Add relative clause pairs (busco un X que + SUBJ vs tengo el X que + IND). After a week, you’ll notice you stop pausing.
Give me those 10 triggers with example sentences, but keep them short and natural.

Sure: 1) Quiero que + SUBJ: Quiero que me llames. 2) Espero que + SUBJ: Espero que llegues bien. 3) Me alegra que + SUBJ: Me alegra que estés aquí. 4) Es importante que + SUBJ: Es importante que lo sepas. 5) No creo que + SUBJ: No creo que tenga tiempo. 6) Dudo que + SUBJ: Dudo que funcione. 7) Es posible que + SUBJ: Es posible que llueva. 8) Antes de que + SUBJ: Antes de que salgas, apaga la luz. 9) Para que + SUBJ: Te lo repito para que lo entiendas. 10) Cuando + future + SUBJ: Cuando termine, te aviso.
Okay. Now contrast each with an indicative version so I see the difference.

Some have clear indicative counterparts; others don’t naturally switch because their meaning is inherently non-assertive. But I’ll give contrasts where it makes sense: 1) Quiero que me llames. (SUBJ desire) vs Sé que me llamas. (IND fact) / Me dices que me llamas. (IND report) 2) Espero que llegues bien. (SUBJ hope) vs Veo que llegas bien. (IND observation) 3) Me alegra que estés aquí. (SUBJ reaction) vs Es cierto que estás aquí. (IND assertion) 4) Es importante que lo sepas. (SUBJ importance) vs Está claro que lo sabes. (IND certainty) 5) No creo que tenga tiempo. (SUBJ lack of belief) vs Creo que tiene tiempo. (IND belief) 6) Dudo que funcione. (SUBJ doubt) vs No dudo que funciona. (IND certainty) 7) Es posible que llueva. (SUBJ possibility) vs Es seguro que llueve / va a llover. (IND certainty) 8) Antes de que salgas… (SUBJ “before”) vs Después de que sales… (habitual) / Después de que saliste… (past fact) (IND) 9) Para que lo entiendas… (SUBJ purpose) vs Porque lo entiendes… (IND reason; different connector) 10) Cuando termine, te aviso. (SUBJ future) vs Cuando termino, te aviso. (IND habitual: whenever I finish, I let you know)
That helps. But you had to change the structure for some. So it’s not a clean opposite.
Right—some triggers don’t have a neat “indicative version” because the meaning itself is what triggers the mood. The “opposite” is often a different predicate that asserts facts.
I need to ask about “maybe” again. “A lo mejor” uses indicative, right?

“A lo mejor” strongly prefers indicative: - A lo mejor viene mañana. - A lo mejor no tienen tiempo. Subjunctive with “a lo mejor” is much less common and often sounds off in many varieties. So if you want a reliable choice: “a lo mejor + IND.”
Great. One less variable.
Exactly—use “a lo mejor” when you want to avoid the tal vez/quizá mood choice.
Another thing: “ojalá.” Always subjunctive, but which tense? “Ojalá viene” is wrong, right?

“Ojalá” takes subjunctive: - Ojalá venga. (hope for future/present) - Ojalá viniera/viniese. (wish, often more hypothetical or less likely; can also be “I wish he would come”) - Ojalá hubiera/hubiese venido. (wish about past not happening) So: - Ojalá venga mañana. (I hope he comes tomorrow.) - Ojalá viniera más a menudo. (I wish he came more often.) - Ojalá hubiera venido ayer. (I wish he had come yesterday.)
Why does “ojalá viniera” sound like “if only he came,” like more distant?

Because imperfect subjunctive often expresses a wish that is more hypothetical, less immediate, or contrary-to-fact depending on context. It’s similar to English “I wish he came” (which English doesn’t really use) versus “I wish he would come” / “If only he came.” Spanish uses tense to encode that distance.
Okay. I think we covered most triggers. But I still mess up in spontaneous speech.

Let’s simulate spontaneous speech with prompts. I’ll give you a situation prompt; you respond with one sentence using a specific structure. Keep it short. 1) You’re asking someone to lower their voice: use “te pido que…” 2) You’re saying you’re happy they’re here: use “me alegra que…” 3) You’re saying you don’t think it’s true: use “no creo que…” 4) You’re saying you’ll do it when you have time: use “cuando…” 5) You’re looking for someone who can help: use “busco a alguien que…”
1) Te pido que bajes la voz. 2) Me alegra que estés aquí. 3) No creo que sea verdad. 4) Cuando tenga tiempo, lo hago. 5) Busco a alguien que pueda ayudarme.

All correct. For #4, also natural: - Cuando tenga tiempo, lo haré. (one-time future) - Cuando tengo tiempo, lo hago. (habitual) Your “lo hago” with “tenga” is okay if you mean “I’ll do it (then),” but “lo haré” fits the future reference more cleanly.
See, even there I get tense mismatch.

That’s a separate issue from mood. Mood choice (“tenga”) is right; the main clause tense is about whether you mean habitual vs future. If you’re promising a future action, future tense is a good default.
Give me 20 more prompts. I want to grind it out.

Here are 20. For each, produce one sentence using the structure indicated. 1) “Es importante que…” 2) “Es posible que…” 3) “No es seguro que…” 4) “Estoy seguro de que…” (indicative) 5) “Dudo que…” 6) “No dudo que…” (indicative) 7) “Antes de que…” 8) “Para que…” 9) “Sin que…” 10) “Aunque…” (hypothetical: even if) 11) “Aunque…” (factual: although) 12) “Ojalá…” 13) “Busco un/una… que…” (non-specific) 14) “Conozco a alguien que…” (specific) 15) “No hay nadie que…” 16) “Si + imperfect subjunctive…, conditional…” (hypothetical) 17) “Como si…” (imperfect subjunctive) 18) “A lo mejor…” (indicative) 19) “Cuando + subjunctive…” (future) 20) “Cuando + indicative…” (habitual)

1) Es importante que llegues temprano. 2) Es posible que no venga. 3) No es seguro que tenga tiempo. 4) Estoy seguro de que tiene tiempo. 5) Dudo que funcione. 6) No dudo que funciona. 7) Antes de que salgas, cierra la puerta. 8) Te lo digo para que lo entiendas. 9) Se fue sin que yo lo viera. 10) Aunque llueva, voy. 11) Aunque llueve, voy. 12) Ojalá pueda hacerlo. 13) Busco un libro que sea corto. 14) Conozco a alguien que habla ruso. 15) No hay nadie que me entienda. 16) Si tuviera más tiempo, estudiaría más. 17) Me mira como si no me conociera. 18) A lo mejor viene más tarde. 19) Cuando termine, te llamo. 20) Cuando termino, te llamo.

This is strong. Minor notes: - #6 “No dudo que funciona” is correct; many speakers might also say “No dudo que funcione” in some contexts, but standard prescriptive grammar prefers indicative because it asserts certainty. Your choice matches the logic we discussed. - #19 “Cuando termine, te llamo” is okay; “te llamaré” would align with future, but present can be used as near-future. Mood is correct. Everything else is right and natural.
I’m still annoyed that #6 can vary. But okay.

If you want to avoid that variation in your own production, you can choose a more unambiguous certainty expression: - “Estoy seguro de que…” (IND) - “No cabe duda de que…” (IND) Or for doubt: - “Dudo que…” (SUBJ) That way you don’t rely on “no dudo que” which can be stylistically variable.
“No cabe duda de que” sounds formal, but “estoy seguro de que” is fine.
Exactly.
One last thing: “the subjunctive in noun clauses” vs “adjective clauses” vs “adverbial clauses.” People talk about that. Is that just categorizing the same thing?

It’s a useful classification: 1) Noun clauses (complement clauses): act like a noun, often after “que” - Quiero que vengas. (object of “quiero”) - Me alegra que estés aquí. (subject-like content of reaction) Mood depends on the main predicate (volition, emotion, doubt, etc.). 2) Adjective clauses (relative clauses): modify a noun - Busco un profesor que hable inglés. Mood depends on specificity/existence/identification. 3) Adverbial clauses: modify the verb by giving time, purpose, condition, concession, etc. - Cuando llegues, avísame. - Para que lo entiendas… Mood depends on whether it’s future/unknown/hypothetical vs habitual/known, and on connector type (purpose, condition, concession). It’s the same underlying idea (assertion vs non-assertion), but the “decision cues” differ by clause type.
That’s actually a clean way to organize it. I wish I’d learned it that way from the start.

If you practice one category at a time, it’s less overwhelming. Many learners master noun clauses first (quiero que, creo que, es importante que), then time clauses (cuando, hasta que), then relative clauses (busco un X que).
I’m going to keep practicing, but I know I’ll still mess up in the moment and then get irritated.

You will mess up; that’s part of automating it. The goal isn’t zero mistakes immediately; it’s reducing hesitation and making your default choices match your intended meaning. If you want, next time you can bring a short text you wrote, and we’ll rewrite it twice: once with the moods you used, and once with the “native-default” moods, so you can see the pattern errors you repeat.
How do I read IPA transcriptions? I see them in dictionaries but they look like hieroglyphs.

IPA can look intimidating because it’s a different alphabet, but it’s basically a “sound spelling” system. The key idea: each IPA symbol corresponds to a specific speech sound, and (in principle) that sound stays the same across words. A practical way to learn it is to focus on: 1) The IPA symbols your dictionaries actually use (often only a subset). 2) The most common English consonants and vowels. 3) How stress, syllables, and “extra marks” (like length or aspiration) are written. To start, which dictionary are you looking at (or can you paste a couple of IPA transcriptions you want to decode)?
I mostly use online dictionaries. I see things like /ˈkɒntrækt/ and /kənˈtrækt/ and also /ˈθɔːt/ and I’m not sure what the slashes mean either.

Great examples, because they show several core IPA ideas. 1) /slashes/ vs [brackets] - / ... / usually means a phonemic transcription: “the sound categories that distinguish words” in that language. It’s a simplified, dictionary-friendly representation. - [ ... ] usually means a phonetic transcription: more detailed, showing finer pronunciation details (exact tongue position, aspiration, etc.). Many dictionaries use / / by default. 2) Stress marks - ˈ before a syllable means primary stress. Example: /ˈkɒntrækt/ has stress on the first syllable: KON-tract. - ˌ means secondary stress (less strong than primary), e.g. /ˌ.../. 3) Your pair: /ˈkɒntrækt/ vs /kənˈtrækt/ These are likely two different words with the same spelling: - the noun “contract” (agreement) often has stress on the first syllable: /ˈkɒntrækt/ (or /ˈkɑːntrækt/ in some dictionary systems). - the verb “to contract” often has stress on the second syllable: /kənˈtrækt/ Notice the vowel in the first syllable changes too: /kɒn-/ vs /kən-/. 4) /ˈθɔːt/ - /θ/ is the “thin” sound (voiceless dental fricative). - /ɔː/ is a long vowel (the colon-like mark ː indicates length). - /t/ is /t/. So that’s “thought,” in a system where that vowel is long. If you want, we can go symbol-by-symbol. For example, do you recognize /k/ and /t/ already, and it’s mainly the vowels and stress marks that confuse you?
Yeah consonants like k t m n s are fine. It’s the vowels and weird symbols: ɒ ə æ ɔː ʊ ɜː and sometimes ʃ ʒ tʃ dʒ. Also I saw /r/ sometimes and sometimes /ɹ/ and sometimes no r.

That’s a very normal set of “first confusing” symbols. Let’s organize them into manageable chunks: (A) consonant symbols that don’t look like normal letters, (B) vowel symbols, (C) “r” issues, and (D) common diacritics and multi-symbol sounds. A) Consonants that look unfamiliar - /ʃ/ = “sh” as in “ship” - /ʒ/ = the “zh” sound in “measure” or “vision” - /tʃ/ = “ch” as in “chip” (it’s written as two symbols because it’s an affricate: stop + fricative) - /dʒ/ = “j” as in “jam” (also an affricate) B) Vowels you listed (common in many dictionary transcriptions) - /æ/ = vowel in “cat” - /ɒ/ = vowel in “lot” (in many British-oriented transcriptions) - /ə/ = schwa, the very common unstressed vowel, like the ‘a’ in “about” (first syllable) or the ‘o’ in “harmony” (second syllable) - /ʊ/ = vowel in “foot” - /ɜː/ = “nurse” vowel in many systems (often long): like the vowel in “bird” /bɜːd/ in non-rhotic dictionary styles, or /bɝd/ in some systems - /ɔː/ = vowel in “thought” in many systems (often long) Important: IPA symbols are tied to sound qualities, not spelling. So “oo” in spelling can be /uː/ (goose) or /ʊ/ (foot), etc. C) /r/ vs /ɹ/ vs no /r/ - In strict IPA, the common English “r” sound is often transcribed as /ɹ/ (an alveolar approximant). - Some dictionaries simplify and write /r/ instead of /ɹ/ even though technically /r/ is often a trill in IPA. - “Sometimes no r” is about whether the dictionary uses a non-rhotic transcription style (where “r” is only written if it’s pronounced before a vowel) vs a rhotic style (where “r” is always written when it’s historically present). Many British dictionaries omit /r/ in words like “car” in isolation, writing /kɑː/ rather than /kɑːr/. Some American dictionaries would show /kɑr/. If you tell me one word where you saw “no r,” I can show how the same word looks in both styles and what it implies. D) Diacritics and multi-symbol sounds - /ː/ length mark: makes a vowel “longer” - /tʃ/ and /dʒ/ are two-symbol affricates, but function like single consonants Would you like a “cheat sheet” mapping for the most common vowel symbols you’ll see in your dictionary, with example words for each?
Yes please. But I’m worried because examples might depend on how people say them. I just want a reliable way to interpret what the dictionary is telling me.

The reliable part is: within a given dictionary, each symbol consistently means the same target sound category. You’re right that actual pronunciation varies, but dictionaries aim for a stable reference system. Here’s a practical “dictionary IPA vowel cheat sheet” for a common set of symbols you mentioned. I’ll describe them by: - typical example word used in teaching materials - a short “how it feels” description (tongue height/frontness, lip rounding) - common confusion points 1) /iː/ (if you encounter it) - Often in “fleece” (like “see”) - High front, lips unrounded, long 2) /ɪ/ - “kit” (like the vowel in “sit”) - High-ish front, short; not as tense as /iː/ 3) /e/ or /eɪ/ (dictionary-dependent) - /e/ often “dress” in some systems, but many English IPA systems use /e/ for “dress” only in narrow transcription; dictionaries often use /e/ or /ɛ/. - /eɪ/ commonly “face” (like “say”), a diphthong 4) /æ/ - “trap” (like “cat”) - Low front, unrounded 5) /ʌ/ - “strut” (like “cup”) - Mid-ish central/back-ish, unrounded; often confused with /ə/, but /ʌ/ is usually stressed while /ə/ is typically unstressed 6) /ə/ (schwa) - “comma” final vowel, “about” first vowel - Central, very reduced, usually unstressed - Key: schwa is about reduction; it shows “this vowel is weak/unstressed” 7) /ɜː/ - “nurse” (like “bird,” “word” in many dictionaries) - Mid central, often long - Often appears with /r/ depending on dictionary style: /bɜːd/ vs /bɝd/ or /bɜːrd/ 8) /uː/ - “goose” (like “two”) - High back, rounded, long 9) /ʊ/ - “foot” (like “put”) - High back-ish, rounded, short; not as tense as /uː/ 10) /ɔː/ - “thought” (like “law” in many systems) - Mid back, rounded, long 11) /ɒ/ (in many British-oriented transcriptions) - “lot” (like “not”) - Low back, rounded; often paired with /ɔː/ as short vs long, though the quality can differ too 12) /ɑː/ (if you see it) - “palm” (like “father” in many systems) - Low back, unrounded, long 13) Diphthongs you’ll often see: - /aɪ/ “price” (like “my”) - /aʊ/ “mouth” (like “now”) - /ɔɪ/ “choice” (like “boy”) - /əʊ/ or /oʊ/ “goat” (like “go”) - /ɪə/ /eə/ /ʊə/ appear in some systems (often in non-rhotic styles): “near,” “square,” “cure” To interpret a dictionary: treat each symbol as a code for a sound class. Your job is not to guess every subtle detail, but to learn what each code means in that dictionary’s system. If you paste 5–10 transcriptions you’ve seen recently, I can decode them and also point out which vowel set your dictionary is using (British-oriented vs American-oriented, and whether it’s rhotic).

Here are some: /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ (educate), /ˈmeʒə/ (measure), /ˈbʊkˌʃɛlf/ (bookshelf), /ˈpɜːpəl/ (purple), /kəˈmjuːnɪkeɪt/ (communicate), /ˈwɒtʃɪŋ/ (watching), /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ (international), /fəˈnɛtɪks/ (phonetics).

Nice set—these show affricates, the /j/ glide, schwa, stress, and secondary stress. I’ll decode each one in a consistent way: stress + syllables + symbol meanings. 1) /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ “educate” - ˈ = primary stress on first syllable: ED- - /e/ = “eh” vowel (in many dictionary systems this is the “dress” vowel) - /d/ normal - /j/ = “y” sound (palatal approximant), like the start of “yes” - /ʊ/ = “foot” vowel - /k/ normal - /eɪ/ = “face” diphthong (like “say”) - /t/ normal So roughly: ED-yuh-kayt, with the /jʊ/ sequence often sounding like “yoo” or “yuh” depending on reduction and the dictionary’s level of detail. 2) /ˈmeʒə/ “measure” - stress on first syllable - /m/ normal - /e/ = “dress” vowel - /ʒ/ = “zh” sound - /ə/ = schwa (unstressed final vowel) So: ME-zhuh. 3) /ˈbʊkˌʃɛlf/ “bookshelf” - ˈ primary stress on /bʊk/ - /bʊk/ = “book” with /ʊ/ - ˌ secondary stress before /ʃɛlf/ (less prominent than primary, but indicates rhythmic structure) - /ʃ/ = “sh” - /ɛ/ = “dress” vowel (some dictionaries use /e/ instead; yours uses /ɛ/ here) - /lf/ = “lf” So: BOOK-shelf, with secondary stress marking. 4) /ˈpɜːpəl/ “purple” - stress on first syllable - /p/ normal - /ɜː/ = “nurse” vowel, long - /p/ normal - /ə/ schwa in the second syllable - /l/ normal In a non-rhotic transcription style, /ɜː/ doesn’t show an /r/ even if the spelling has one. So it’s “PUR-puhl” (dictionary style), not necessarily telling you to omit any sound in all contexts—just using a system where /r/ isn’t written in that position. 5) /kəˈmjuːnɪkeɪt/ “communicate” - /kə/ = /k/ + schwa, unstressed “kuh” - ˈ stress on /mjuː/: “MYOO” - /m/ normal - /j/ = “y” - /uː/ = long “goose” vowel - /nɪ/ = /n/ + /ɪ/ “nih” - /k/ normal - /eɪ/ “ay” - /t/ normal So: kuh-MYOO-nih-kayt. 6) /ˈwɒtʃɪŋ/ “watching” - stress on first syllable - /w/ normal - /ɒ/ “lot” vowel (in that system) - /tʃ/ “ch” - /ɪ/ “kit” - /ŋ/ = “ng” sound (velar nasal), like the end of “sing” So: WOTCH-ing (with that “lot” vowel). 7) /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ “international” - ˌ secondary stress on /ɪn/: indicates a weaker stress at the start - /ɪn/ = “in” - /tə/ = /t/ + schwa, “tuh” - ˈ primary stress on /næ/ - /næ/ = “na” with /æ/ - /ʃ/ “sh” - /ə/ schwa - /nəl/ = “nuhl” (schwa + /l/) So: in-tuh-NASH-uh-nuhl, with primary stress on -na-. 8) /fəˈnɛtɪks/ “phonetics” - /fə/ = /f/ + schwa, “fuh” (unstressed) - ˈ stress on /nɛ/ - /n/ normal - /ɛ/ “dress” vowel - /tɪks/ = /t/ + /ɪ/ + /k/ + /s/ So: fuh-NET-iks. Two extra takeaways from your data: - Your dictionary uses /ɛ/ and /ɒ/, which is typical of a British-oriented vowel set. - It uses a non-rhotic style in “purple” (/pɜːpəl/ without /r/). Do you want to go deeper into vowels (how to “place” them in the mouth), or into the symbols like /j/ and /ŋ/ and why they’re written that way?
Vowels. I want to look at /ɒ/ and /ɔː/ and /ʊ/ and /uː/ and actually know what I’m supposed to do with my mouth. Also what does “rounded” mean exactly?

“Rounded” refers to what your lips do: rounding means your lips form a more circular opening (like when you whistle), while unrounded means the lips are more spread or neutral. For vowels, three main “controls” shape the sound: 1) Tongue height (high/close vs low/open) 2) Tongue frontness (front vs central vs back) 3) Lip rounding (rounded vs unrounded) Plus sometimes: 4) Tenseness/length (tense vs lax; long vs short) Let’s do your four vowels in pairs, because each pair is closely related: Pair 1: /ʊ/ vs /uː/ - Both are “back-ish” and rounded. - /uː/ (as in “goose”): tongue is high (close) and toward the back; lips rounded; usually longer and “tenser.” - /ʊ/ (as in “foot”): tongue is also high but typically a bit lower and more central than /uː/; lips rounded but often less tightly; usually shorter and “laxer.” Mouth instruction: - For /uː/: start with a fairly small mouth opening, tongue high, lips noticeably rounded. Hold it. - For /ʊ/: relax it slightly—less tight lip rounding, slightly more open jaw, shorter duration. Common learner issue: making /ʊ/ too close to /uː/. If they sound identical, you lose contrasts like “full” vs “fool” (in systems that distinguish them clearly). Pair 2: /ɒ/ vs /ɔː/ - Both are “back” and usually rounded. - /ɒ/ (often “lot”): lower (more open) tongue position; jaw more open; short. - /ɔː/ (often “thought”): higher than /ɒ/ (mid-open rather than open), rounded, and long. Mouth instruction: - For /ɒ/: open your jaw more; keep the tongue low and back; lips can be rounded but often not extremely tight. - For /ɔː/: close the jaw slightly compared to /ɒ/; tongue a bit higher; lips rounded; hold it longer. What “long” means in /ɔː/: - The ː indicates length. It’s not just “drag it out dramatically,” but there is a consistent difference in duration in careful speech, and it also often correlates with vowel quality (a slightly different tongue/lip setting). If you want a concrete drill: - Alternate /uː ʊ uː ʊ/ while keeping everything else constant. - Alternate /ɔː ɒ ɔː ɒ/ similarly. Record yourself and listen for two different “targets.” One more important note: dictionaries use a standard symbol system, but real-world vowels can vary by region and speaker. Still, the articulatory targets I described (high vs slightly lower, more tense vs less tense, etc.) are consistent enough to be useful for learning the contrasts. Do you want me to describe these using the IPA vowel chart terms (close/open, front/back), or do you prefer purely practical “do this with tongue/lips/jaw” instructions?
Use the vowel chart terms too. I’ve seen the trapezoid but I don’t know how to read it.

The IPA vowel chart (the “trapezoid”) is a map of tongue position, not a map of where sounds come out of the mouth. How to read it: 1) Vertical axis: tongue height - Top = close/high vowels (tongue close to the roof of the mouth) - Bottom = open/low vowels (tongue lower, mouth more open) 2) Horizontal axis: tongue frontness - Left = front vowels (tongue body pushed forward) - Right = back vowels (tongue body pulled back) 3) Rounding: left/right pairs at the same spot In the official IPA chart, many vowel positions have two symbols: - Left symbol = unrounded - Right symbol = rounded So rounding is represented by which symbol you choose, not by a separate mark (usually). Now, where your vowels tend to sit: /uː/ (goose): - Close (high), back, rounded. Near the top-right area. /ʊ/ (foot): - Near-close (slightly lower than /uː/), near-back, rounded. Slightly down and a bit left from /uː/. /ɔː/ (thought): - Open-mid to mid, back, rounded. Mid-right area, lower than /o/ and higher than /ɒ/. /ɒ/ (lot): - Open (low), back, rounded. Lower-right area. A subtlety: the IPA chart has cardinal vowels like [u], [o], [ɔ], [ɑ], [ɒ]. English dictionary symbols /uː ʊ ɔː ɒ/ are approximations of those areas, but they’re language-specific categories, not exact cardinal vowel points. How the trapezoid helps you: - If two vowels are far apart on the chart, they should sound quite different. - If they’re close together (like /uː/ and /ʊ/), your goal is a small but consistent difference. Quick exercise using the chart idea: - /uː/ vs /ʊ/: move slightly down (more open) and slightly forward (less back) + less tense. - /ɔː/ vs /ɒ/: move down (more open) for /ɒ/, and up (less open) for /ɔː/ + longer. Do you want to add two more vowels to anchor the chart—like /iː/ (top-left) and /ɑː/ (bottom-right-ish)—so the map feels less abstract?
Yes. And also /æ/ and /ɜː/ because I see them a lot.

Good anchors. Let’s place /iː/, /ɑː/, /æ/, /ɜː/ on the same map and relate them to mouth positions. 1) /iː/ (often “fleece”) - Close/high, front, unrounded. - Tongue high and forward; lips spread/neutral. - Top-left of the chart. 2) /ɑː/ (often “palm”) - Open/low, back (or sometimes central-back depending on the system), unrounded. - Jaw open; tongue low; lips usually unrounded (more neutral than /ɒ/). - Bottom-right-ish (but unrounded). 3) /æ/ (often “trap”) - Near-open (quite low), front, unrounded. - Jaw fairly open; tongue low and forward. - Lower-left area. 4) /ɜː/ (often “nurse”) - Mid, central, usually long. - Tongue in the center area; lips neutral; steady quality. - Center of chart (mid-central). Now compare them to what you already have: - /iː/ vs /uː/: both high, but /iː/ is front unrounded; /uː/ is back rounded. - /æ/ vs /ɒ/: both low-ish, but /æ/ is front unrounded; /ɒ/ is back rounded. - /ɜː/ is central; it’s not strongly front or back, and lip rounding is typically neutral. Practical “feel” tips: - /iː/: you often feel the tongue high near the hard palate; lips slightly spread. - /æ/: you may feel the tongue forward and the mouth open; it can feel “wide.” - /ɜː/: relaxed mouth; tongue not extreme; it’s a “steady central” sound. - /ɑː/: open jaw; tongue low; not rounded like /ɒ/. One caution: /ɜː/ is tricky because in many contexts it’s associated with an “r” in spelling, but in non-rhotic dictionary styles you’ll see /ɜː/ without /r/ unless a vowel follows. So the vowel category is central; whether an /r/-like sound appears depends on the transcription style and phonological environment. If you want, we can do a “chart tour” by taking a few words and seeing how their vowels jump around: e.g., /iː/ in “see,” /æ/ in “cat,” /ɜː/ in “bird,” /ɑː/ in “father,” /ɒ/ in “not,” /ɔː/ in “thought,” /ʊ/ in “foot,” /uː/ in “food.” That gives you a mental map.
That helps. Now, what about those little marks: I saw a dot like /ˈbɪt.ə/ or sometimes a small raised h like /pʰ/ and weird little lines under letters.

Those are diacritics and separators—IPA’s way of adding detail. 1) The dot /./ - A dot between symbols often marks a syllable boundary. Example: /ˈbɪt.ə/ means two syllables: BIT-ə. Dictionaries vary: some use dots, some don’t; some use /ˈbɪtə/ without marking the boundary. 2) The small raised h: /ʰ/ (aspiration) - /pʰ tʰ kʰ/ indicate aspirated stops: a puff of air after the consonant release. - In English, aspiration happens mainly when /p t k/ start a stressed syllable (like the first sound in “pin,” “top,” “cat”). - In broad dictionary transcription, aspiration is often not shown because it’s predictable. In narrower transcription, it might be. How to test aspiration: hold your hand in front of your mouth and say a word starting with /p/; you can feel the puff. 3) Little lines under letters: these can be different things depending on the exact mark: - A small line under a consonant can indicate it’s “syllabic,” e.g. [n̩] or [l̩]. That means the consonant itself forms the syllable nucleus (like the second syllable in some pronunciations of “bottle” where the /l/ can be syllabic). - Under a vowel, a diacritic can indicate “more central” or “less rounded,” etc., depending on the mark. IPA has many diacritics. Common ones you might see: - [ː] length mark (you already saw) - [ˑ] half-long (less common in dictionaries) - [̃] nasalization (more common in languages like French; in English you might see it in detailed phonetics) - [ʔ] glottal stop (sometimes used for certain /t/ realizations in narrow transcription) If you can type the exact symbol with the line under it (or paste the transcription), I can tell you exactly which diacritic it is and what it means.
I saw something like [l̩] and [n̩] in a pronunciation guide. And I also saw [ɫ] for dark l. Are those in IPA too?

Yes, those are standard IPA symbols/diacritics. 1) Syllabic consonants: [l̩] and [n̩] - The small vertical mark under the consonant ( ̩ ) means “syllabic.” - [l̩] = syllabic L: the /l/ itself acts like the vowel of that syllable. - [n̩] = syllabic N. Why it happens: in some contexts, especially in unstressed syllables, the vowel can reduce so much that the consonant becomes the syllable peak. Example pattern (not focusing on any specific variety): words ending in -le or -en often can be analyzed with syllabic [l̩] or [n̩] in phonetic transcription. 2) [ɫ] “dark L” - [ɫ] is a velarized alveolar lateral approximant. - “Velarized” means the back of the tongue is raised toward the velum (soft palate) at the same time as you make an /l/. - In many descriptions of English, /l/ has a “clear” version [l] and a “dark” version [ɫ] depending on position in the syllable (often darker in coda positions). So yes: [ɫ] is an IPA symbol. Some transcriptions use [l] with a diacritic [lˠ] instead; [ɫ] is a convenient single character used widely. Key difference between / / and [ ] here: - A dictionary might say the phoneme is /l/ (broad). - A phonetics guide might show [l] vs [ɫ] (narrow), because it’s describing allophonic detail. Do you want a quick way to recognize when a transcription is “broad” vs “narrow” at a glance?
Yes, because sometimes I don’t know if I’m supposed to copy every detail.

A quick checklist: Signs it’s broad (phonemic, “dictionary level”): - Uses /slashes/. - Fewer diacritics. - Often doesn’t mark predictable details like aspiration, dark/light L, nasalization before nasal consonants, etc. - Focus is on contrasts that change word identity. Signs it’s narrow (phonetic, “exact-ish”): - Uses [square brackets]. - Includes diacritics like [ʰ], [̩], [̃], [ˠ], [̥], etc. - May show assimilation, flapping, glottal stops, devoicing, and other context effects. What should you copy? - If your goal is to pronounce words intelligibly, start with broad transcription: get consonant and vowel categories and stress right. - Use narrow details only if you’re doing phonetics study, or refining a specific sound pattern. A practical rule: if you see [ ] and lots of little marks, treat it as descriptive detail, not a mandatory “must do every micro-gesture” instruction. But it can still be useful: it tells you what often happens in natural speech. If you want, paste a narrow transcription you saw and we can decide which parts are essential for a learner and which are optional detail.
Okay. Another thing: dictionaries sometimes show two pronunciations, like /ˈdeɪtə/ and /ˈdɑːtə/ for the same word. Is IPA supposed to have one correct form?

IPA itself doesn’t enforce “one correct form.” It’s a notation system. Multiple pronunciations can be equally standard or common, and dictionaries often list alternatives. When a dictionary shows two IPA forms, it’s usually because: - There are two established pronunciations in common use. - The word has variant stress patterns, vowel choices, or consonant differences. - The dictionary is trying to cover different standard conventions. How to interpret it practically: - Both are acceptable targets (assuming the dictionary is reputable). - Choose one and be consistent, especially within the same word family if possible. - Pay attention to whether the variants differ only in vowel quality/length, or also in stress—stress differences can affect intelligibility more. IPA is not a “single truth”; it’s a tool to represent speech. The “correctness” comes from social convention and the dictionary’s chosen standards, not from IPA. Do you want to understand what the symbols in your example mean (/ˈdeɪtə/ vs /ˈdɑːtə/), or are you more interested in how dictionaries decide which variants to list?
Explain the symbols there and what the difference is. Also, why does one have /eɪ/ and the other /ɑː/?

/ˈdeɪtə/ vs /ˈdɑːtə/ differ in the vowel of the first syllable. - ˈ = stress on the first syllable in both. - /d/ = the consonant “d”. - /eɪ/ = the “face” diphthong, like the vowel in “day.” It starts around [e] and glides toward [ɪ]. So /ˈdeɪtə/ is roughly “DAY-tə” (with schwa /ə/ in the second syllable). - /ɑː/ = a long open back unrounded vowel, like “palm” in many dictionary systems. So /ˈdɑːtə/ is roughly “DAH-tə” (longer, more open, not a glide). Why one has /eɪ/ and the other /ɑː/: - They represent two different established vowel choices for the same word. IPA is just encoding that difference. - The dictionary is telling you: some speakers use the “face” diphthong, others use the “palm” long vowel. The important thing is: /eɪ/ is a diphthong (movement), and /ɑː/ is a monophthong (steady vowel) plus length. If you want to get comfortable with diphthongs, we can go through the common ones and how to “read” them: /eɪ aɪ ɔɪ aʊ əʊ/ etc.
Yes, diphthongs confuse me. When I see /aɪ/ I don’t know how to blend them. Do I pronounce /a/ then /ɪ/ separately?

Think of a diphthong as one vowel that moves, not two separate vowels in sequence. How to read /aɪ/: - It starts with a vowel quality near [a] (open, front-ish/central-ish, unrounded). - Then it glides toward [ɪ] (near-close front, unrounded). - You don’t “reset” the mouth; you smoothly shift tongue position during the same syllable. A practical method: 1) Start by saying the first target and holding it briefly: [a...] 2) Then glide toward the second target quickly: [...ɪ] 3) Over time, make it one smooth movement: [aɪ] Same idea for other diphthongs: /eɪ/ = [e] gliding to [ɪ] /ɔɪ/ = [ɔ] gliding to [ɪ] /aʊ/ = [a] gliding to [ʊ] /əʊ/ or /oʊ/ = [ə] or [o] gliding to [ʊ] Important: the second element is usually shorter and less prominent; the “identity” of the diphthong is often carried by the starting point. If you want, we can practice reading diphthongs in context: I can give you a list of IPA words and you can tell me how you think they sound, then I’ll correct the interpretation.
Give me a list. But keep it beginner-friendly and include stress marks.

Here are 12 beginner-friendly items focusing on common diphthongs and stress. I’ll keep the consonants mostly simple: 1) /ˈdeɪ/ 2) /ˈseɪ/ 3) /ˈtaɪm/ 4) /ˈraɪs/ 5) /ˈbɔɪ/ 6) /ˈtɔɪ/ 7) /ˈnaʊ/ 8) /ˈhaʊs/ 9) /ˈgəʊ/ 10) /ˈhəʊm/ 11) /ˈfɪə/ 12) /ˈpeə/ Your task: for each, say what word you think it represents (or just approximate it in normal spelling). Then we’ll check the diphthong reading: /eɪ aɪ ɔɪ aʊ əʊ ɪə eə/.
1 day, 2 say, 3 time, 4 rice, 5 boy, 6 toy, 7 now, 8 house, 9 go, 10 home, 11 fear?, 12 pair?

That’s correct across the set, and your guesses for 11 and 12 are exactly the intended words: “fear” and “pair.” Now, what these show about diphthongs: - /deɪ seɪ/ “day, say”: /eɪ/ starts mid-front and glides up toward /ɪ/. - /taɪm raɪs/ “time, rice”: /aɪ/ starts open and glides up/front. - /bɔɪ tɔɪ/ “boy, toy”: /ɔɪ/ starts with a rounded back-ish vowel and glides to /ɪ/. - /naʊ haʊs/ “now, house”: /aʊ/ starts open and glides toward /ʊ/ (rounded, higher). - /gəʊ həʊm/ “go, home”: /əʊ/ starts central-ish and glides to /ʊ/. Some dictionaries write /oʊ/ instead. - /fɪə peə/ “fear, pair”: these are sometimes analyzed as diphthongs (especially in non-rhotic transcription styles) because there’s a glide toward a central vowel. In rhotic systems you might see an /r/ element (like /fɪr/ or /fɪər/ depending on notation). So you’re already reading them correctly. Would you like to move to consonant symbols that behave differently than spelling (like /θ ð ŋ ʃ ʒ/), or to stress and syllable patterns (like why /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ has two stress marks)?
Stress and syllables. I don’t know where to put stress from IPA, and I don’t know what secondary stress really does.

Stress in IPA is mainly about prominence: one syllable stands out as the main “beat” (primary stress), and sometimes another syllable has a smaller beat (secondary stress), especially in longer words. 1) Primary stress ˈ - The syllable immediately after ˈ is the stressed one. Example: /fəˈnɛtɪks/ → stress on /nɛ/: pho-NET-ics. What changes in a stressed syllable? - Often louder, longer, and/or higher in pitch. - The vowel is usually “full” (not schwa), while unstressed vowels often reduce to /ə/ or /ɪ/ depending on the system. 2) Secondary stress ˌ - A weaker kind of stress that helps rhythm and can prevent too many reduced syllables in a row. Example: /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ - primary stress on /næ/ - secondary stress on /ɪn/ at the beginning What does it do practically? - You give that syllable a bit more clarity: slightly more length or pitch movement than fully unstressed syllables. - But it should not compete with the primary stress. 3) Syllable boundaries - Sometimes marked with dots: /ˌɪn.təˈnæʃ.ə.nəl/ (some transcriptions would show it like that). Even without dots, you can often infer syllables from vowel positions: each vowel (or syllabic consonant) typically corresponds to a syllable nucleus. 4) A practical stress-finding method from IPA: - Locate ˈ: that’s your main stressed syllable. - Clap or tap on that syllable as you say the word. - Reduce other syllables: if you see /ə/, it’s a strong hint that syllable is unstressed. Try it with your earlier examples: - /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ → ED-... - /kəˈmjuːnɪkeɪt/ → ...-MYOO-... - /fəˈnɛtɪks/ → ...-NET-... If you want, we can take a longer word you care about and mark its syllables explicitly, then practice where the stress goes.
Let’s do “international” and “communication” (or communicate). I always mess up the stress.

Sure. Using the transcriptions you provided: 1) “international” /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ Let’s segment it by vowels: - /ˌɪn/ (has vowel /ɪ/) → syllable 1, secondary stress - /tə/ (has vowel /ə/) → syllable 2, unstressed - /ˈnæ/ (has vowel /æ/) → syllable 3, primary stress - /ʃə/ (has vowel /ə/) → syllable 4, unstressed - /nəl/ (has vowel /ə/ + /l/) → syllable 5, unstressed (often ends with a syllabic /l/ or a schwa+L sequence depending on transcription) So rhythmically: IN-tə-NASH-ə-nəl Primary stress: NASH Secondary stress: IN Practical tip: keep the schwas very light. The stressed /æ/ should be clear and open. 2) “communicate” /kəˈmjuːnɪkeɪt/ Segment by vowels: - /kə/ → syllable 1, unstressed (schwa) - /ˈmjuː/ → syllable 2, primary stress - /nɪ/ → syllable 3, unstressed (but has /ɪ/, not schwa—still lighter than the stressed syllable) - /keɪt/ → syllable 4, unstressed in this transcription (some speakers may give it a bit of prominence; but primary stress is clearly on /mjuː/) Rhythm: kə-MYOO-ni-kayt Practical tip: don’t over-stress the final /-keɪt/ even though it has a full diphthong /eɪ/. English can have unstressed syllables with full vowels, especially in certain suffixes or word patterns. If you want to go further: “communication” has more syllables and often secondary stress. If you can provide the IPA your dictionary gives for “communication,” we can interpret it accurately.
My dictionary shows “communication” as /kəˌmjuːnɪˈkeɪʃən/.

Perfect. Let’s decode it: /kəˌmjuːnɪˈkeɪʃən/ Step 1: identify stresses - No ˈ until later, so primary stress is on /keɪ/. - There is a ˌ before /mjuː/: secondary stress on that syllable. Step 2: syllable segmentation by vowel nuclei - /kə/ = syllable 1 (schwa, unstressed) - /ˌmjuː/ = syllable 2 (secondary stress) - /nɪ/ = syllable 3 (unstressed) - /ˈkeɪ/ = syllable 4 (primary stress) - /ʃən/ = syllable 5 (schwa in the last syllable) So: kə-MYOO-ni-KAY-shən Primary: KAY Secondary: MYOO Practical rhythm: the main “peak” is on -KAY-, but -MYOO- has a smaller beat so the word doesn’t become a long string of weak syllables. Also note the suffix /-ʃən/: - /ʃ/ “sh” - /ə/ schwa - /n/ n Many words ending in “-tion” or “-sion” have /ʃən/ or /ʒən/ depending on the word. Do you want a mini-guide to common spelling-to-IPA patterns like -tion /ʃən/, -sion /ʒən/, -ture /tʃə/ (in some words), etc., or do you want to stay strictly with “how to read IPA” rather than predict it from spelling?
A mini-guide would be useful, but keep it focused on reading IPA rather than spelling rules.

Here are common IPA chunks you’ll see in dictionaries that correspond to frequent endings. The goal is: when you see the chunk, you immediately know the sound, without needing to analyze symbol by symbol every time. 1) /ʃən/ and /ʒən/ - /ʃən/ = “shən” (as in the end of “communication” /...ˈkeɪʃən/) - /ʒən/ = “zhən” (appears in words like “vision” /ˈvɪʒən/ in many transcriptions) 2) /tʃə/ and /dʒə/ (less universal, but you may see them) - /tʃə/ = “chə” - /dʒə/ = “jə” Sometimes appears in words with “-ture” or “-dure” type endings in certain analyses, but dictionaries vary. 3) /-ɪŋ/ - /ɪŋ/ = “-ing” with /ŋ/ at the end: /ˈwɒtʃɪŋ/ “watching” 4) /-əl/ and /-ən/ with schwa - /əl/ = schwa + L - /ən/ = schwa + N Often in endings that sound like “-le” or “-en” in many words. 5) /-əti/ or /-ɪti/ - /əti/ is like “uh-tee” (schwa + /t/ + /i/) - /ɪti/ is like “ih-tee” Dictionaries differ on whether they use /i/ or /iː/ in that final vowel; some use /i/ to mean a short “happy” vowel. 6) /-eɪt/ - /eɪt/ = “-ate” as in /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ “educate” When you see these chunks, you can treat them like “macros”: recognize /ʃən/ instantly, instead of reading each symbol anew. If you tell me which endings you see most often in your reading (like /-ʃən/, /-ʒən/, /-ɪst/, /-ɪzm/), I can list the most common IPA chunks and what they sound like.
I also see /-ɪst/ and /-ɪzəm/ and /-iː/ vs /-i/ at the end of words. What’s the deal with /i/ without the colon?

Good observation—word-final “i” is one of the biggest dictionary-notation differences. 1) /-ɪst/ and /-ɪzəm/ - /ɪst/ = “ist” with the “kit” vowel /ɪ/ (e.g., “artist” ends with something like /-ɪst/ in many transcriptions) - /ɪzəm/ = “izm” with /ɪ/ then schwa /ə/ (e.g., /-ɪzəm/) 2) /iː/ vs /i/ at the end Different dictionaries handle the final vowel in words like “happy,” “coffee,” “city,” etc. in different ways: - Some dictionaries use /iː/ to represent that final vowel, treating it as the same as the “fleece” vowel. - Many modern dictionaries use /i/ (without length mark) to represent a “happy vowel”: a close front unrounded vowel that is often shorter and slightly different in quality than /iː/, and whose length may vary by context. So /i/ at the end is not “the short /ɪ/.” It’s a separate convention: a symbol for a vowel that is typically close to [i] but not necessarily as long/tense as /iː/ in stressed positions. Practical takeaway: - If you see /i/ word-finally, pronounce it like a relaxed “ee” sound, not like /ɪ/. - If you see /iː/, pronounce a clearer, longer “ee.” But again, the exact boundary depends on the dictionary’s system. The important thing for reading is: /i/ is a vowel symbol, not a consonant, and it’s not the same as /ɪ/. If you want, I can show you a few minimal contrasts in transcription that clarify /i/ vs /ɪ/ vs /iː/ and how dictionaries use them.
Yes. And also, why does /ə/ appear so much? Is it just “uh”?

Let’s do both. A) /iː/ vs /ɪ/ vs /i/ - /iː/ = close front unrounded, long/tense, usually stressed or capable of stress: think “see” /siː/ - /ɪ/ = near-close near-front unrounded, short/lax: “sit” /sɪt/ - /i/ (often “happy vowel”) = close front unrounded, typically unstressed, variable length: “happy” /ˈhæpi/ in dictionaries that use it So: - /ˈsɪti/ “city” ends with /i/ in many systems, not /iː/ and not /ɪ/. - /ˈsiːti/ would suggest a stressed “see” vowel at the end, which is not typical in normal English stress patterns. B) Why /ə/ appears so much /ə/ (schwa) is extremely common because English reduces vowels in unstressed syllables a lot. Is it just “uh”? - It’s a central, neutral vowel sound, often like a very relaxed “uh.” - But the key feature is not exactly its “quality” but its role: it marks an unstressed, reduced vowel. - It can correspond to many different spellings: a, e, o, u, even some cases of i/y in unstressed positions. So when you see /ə/ in IPA, the dictionary is telling you: “this syllable is unstressed and the vowel is reduced.” Practical use: - If you pronounce every written vowel as a full vowel, you’ll often sound unnatural and may put stress in the wrong place. - Learning to recognize /ə/ helps you place stress correctly. Try reading this pair: - /ˈfəʊtəʊ/ (photo) vs /fəˈtɒgrəfi/ (photography) in some systems (transcriptions vary) You’d see schwas appear where vowels reduce in the longer word. If you want, give me a few words you often mis-stress, and we’ll look at their IPA and see where schwa is telling you to weaken syllables.
Before that, can you explain consonant pairs like /s/ vs /z/ and /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/? I can’t always hear the difference in IPA.

These pairs are about voicing and place/manner. 1) /s/ vs /z/ (same place/manner, different voicing) - Both are alveolar fricatives: tongue near the alveolar ridge, air forced through a narrow channel creating friction. - /s/ is voiceless: vocal folds not vibrating. - /z/ is voiced: vocal folds vibrating. How to feel it: - Put fingers lightly on your throat. - Say /ssss/ (no buzz). - Say /zzzz/ (you should feel buzzing). 2) /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/ (same place/manner, different voicing) - Both are postalveolar fricatives (often called “palato-alveolar”): tongue slightly further back than /s z/, with a different tongue shape. - /ʃ/ is voiceless (“sh”). - /ʒ/ is voiced (“zh”). Feel test: - /ʃʃʃʃ/ no buzz - /ʒʒʒʒ/ buzz Hearing difference: - Voiced sounds (/z ʒ/) often sound “richer” or “buzzier.” - Voiceless (/s ʃ/) sound “hissier.” In IPA, the symbol difference is telling you voicing: - /s/ vs /z/ are a voiceless/voiced pair. - /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/ are a voiceless/voiced pair. If you want to connect them to spelling patterns without overdoing rules: many plural endings are /s/ or /z/ depending on voicing of the previous sound, but dictionaries just show which one occurs in each word. Want a short list of minimal pairs in IPA to practice reading /s/ vs /z/ and /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/?
Yes, minimal pairs in IPA would be great. But keep them simple.

Here are simple minimal pairs (same except for the target sound). Read them and focus on the contrast: /s/ vs /z/ 1) /sɪp/ vs /zɪp/ 2) /bʌs/ vs /bʌz/ 3) /siː/ vs /ziː/ 4) /raɪs/ vs /raɪz/ /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/ 5) /ʃɪp/ vs /ʒɪp/ (the second is less common as a word, but it’s a clean sound contrast) 6) /ˈmeʒə/ vs /ˈmeʃə/ (again, one may not be a common word, but it shows the sound difference) 7) /ˈvɪʒən/ vs /ˈvɪʃən/ (same idea) /s/ vs /ʃ/ (place contrast) 8) /sɪp/ vs /ʃɪp/ /z/ vs /ʒ/ (place + voicing) 9) /zɪp/ vs /ʒɪp/ Even when the “word” isn’t common, minimal pairs are useful because they isolate the sound contrast. If you want only real everyday words, we can do contrast sets rather than perfect minimal pairs (because /ʒ/ is rare word-initially). For example: “sip” /sɪp/, “zip” /zɪp/, “ship” /ʃɪp/, “measure” /ˈmeʒə/.
That’s fine. Now, can we go back to /r/? I see /r/ in some dictionaries and not in others, and it confuses me when I’m trying to pronounce from IPA.

The core issue is transcription style: rhotic vs non-rhotic representation, plus symbol choice /r/ vs /ɹ/. 1) /r/ vs /ɹ/ - Strict IPA often uses /ɹ/ for the common English “r” sound (an approximant). - Many dictionaries use /r/ as a simpler stand-in. So /r/ in a dictionary often means [ɹ] in actual articulation. So don’t worry too much: in many dictionary contexts, /r/ and /ɹ/ are functionally the same symbol choice. 2) Why “r” sometimes disappears Some dictionaries use a non-rhotic transcription convention: - They write /r/ only when it occurs before a vowel sound in the same word or across word boundaries (in connected speech). - They omit /r/ at the end of a syllable if it’s not followed by a vowel. So a word like “car” might be shown as /kɑː/ in such a dictionary, while another dictionary might show /kɑːr/ or /kɑr/. How to use it practically: - If your dictionary omits /r/ in places, it’s not telling you “never pronounce an r-like sound in any context.” It’s reflecting a particular reference pronunciation system. - If your dictionary includes /r/ everywhere it historically appears, it’s using a rhotic transcription style. A helpful approach when learning: - Pick one dictionary and stick to its system while you’re learning IPA. - Learn its conventions: does it write /r/ after vowels in “car,” “bird,” “turn”? If not, it’s non-rhotic style. If you paste two entries for a word with “r” from two dictionaries, I can show how to translate between the notations so you’re not thrown off.
I saw “bird” as /bɜːd/ in one place and /bɝd/ in another. What is /ɝ/?

/ɝ/ is a symbol used in some transcription systems (especially in American-oriented ones) to represent an r-colored vowel: essentially a stressed “nurse” vowel with an /r/-quality built into it. - /ɜː/ (as in /bɜːd/) is a mid-central long vowel. In a non-rhotic system, it appears without /r/ even if spelling has r, because the /r/ isn’t written in that position in that system. - /ɝ/ represents a rhotic vowel quality (often analyzed as /ɜ/ + /r/ combined). It’s typically used for stressed syllables. There’s also /ɚ/ for the unstressed version (like the second syllable of “teacher” in some systems). So: - /bɜːd/ vs /bɝd/ are two different notation traditions for essentially the same lexical set, with different assumptions about rhoticity and r-coloring. Reading rule: - If you see /ɝ/, treat it as “the stressed ‘nurse’ vowel with r-coloring.” It’s one sound unit in that system. - If you see /ɜːr/ (or /ɜː/ with /r/ elsewhere), treat it as vowel + /r/ sequence depending on the system. If your goal is simply to pronounce words from the dictionary you’re using, you can stay within one system: learn /ɜː/ if that’s what you see, or learn /ɝ/ if that’s what you see.
Okay. Another symbol: I sometimes see /ɾ/ in phonetic transcriptions. Is that the same as /t/?

/ɾ/ is the IPA symbol for an alveolar tap (or flap). It’s not the same as /t/ (a voiceless alveolar stop), but it can be a common phonetic realization of /t/ (and sometimes /d/) in certain contexts. Difference: - /t/ is a stop: complete closure, then release. - /ɾ/ is a quick tap: the tongue quickly strikes the alveolar ridge and rebounds; it’s very brief, more like a single contact than a full stop closure. In many phonetic descriptions of English, /ɾ/ appears as an allophone in certain intervocalic contexts. In broad dictionary IPA you usually still see /t/ or /d/ because those are the phonemes; in narrow transcription you might see [ɾ]. So, when you see /ɾ/: - It’s a specific consonant sound (tap). - It often indicates a particular phonetic outcome rather than a different word. If you want, I can show how a broad /t/ might become [tʰ], [t], [ɾ], or [ʔ] depending on context in narrow transcription—useful for understanding why your sources differ.
Yes, but keep it as “how to read it,” not a whole course. I just want to know what the symbols mean when I see them.

Understood—here’s a “symbol meaning” map for common /t/ variants you might encounter: 1) [tʰ] - /t/ with aspiration (ʰ). Meaning: a puff of air after the release. - Often appears at the start of a stressed syllable in narrow transcription. 2) [t] (plain) - Unaspirated /t/ (no special diacritic). Could occur after /s/ or in other contexts where aspiration is reduced. 3) [ɾ] - Alveolar tap/flap. Meaning: a quick single contact, not a full stop. - Often used in narrow transcription for certain intervocalic /t/ or /d/ realizations. 4) [ʔ] - Glottal stop. Meaning: closure at the vocal folds. - Sometimes used in narrow transcription to represent a glottal replacement or reinforcement of /t/ in some contexts. 5) [t̚] - Unreleased /t/ (diacritic ̚).
