Meaning: the tongue makes the closure but you don’t release it audibly. - Often occurs at the end of syllables/words in narrow transcription. So if you see a [t] with extra marks, each mark is telling you “what kind of t-like sound” it is phonetically. Broad vs narrow again: - Broad: /t/ is enough. - Narrow: you’ll see [tʰ], [ɾ], [t̚], etc. If you paste the exact transcription containing /ɾ/ that you saw, I can gloss it symbol-by-symbol so you can read it quickly.
I saw something like [ˈbɛɾɚ] for a word. And also [ˈlɪɾəl]. I guess those are “better” and “little”?

Yes, those are classic examples in narrow transcription. 1) [ˈbɛɾɚ] - ˈ = primary stress on first syllable - [b] = b - [ɛ] = “dress” vowel (as in “bet”) - [ɾ] = alveolar tap (a quick “t/d-like” tap) - [ɚ] = r-colored schwa (unstressed rhotic vowel) So this is “better” with a tap in the middle and a rhotic ending vowel. 2) [ˈlɪɾəl] - ˈ stress on first syllable - [l] = l - [ɪ] = “kit” vowel - [ɾ] = tap - [ə] = schwa - [l] = l So “little” with a tap and a schwa-like second syllable (some transcriptions might show syllabic [l̩] instead of [əl]). Reading lesson: - The appearance of [ɾ] tells you “this is a tap,” often corresponding to a /t/ or /d/ phoneme in broad transcription. - [ɚ] tells you “unstressed r-colored vowel.” So yes: you’re interpreting them correctly.
This is starting to make sense. But I still get stuck when I see symbols like /ɡ/ vs /g/ and /ɾ/ vs /r/. Are those different characters or just fonts?

Great question—IPA uses specific Unicode characters, and fonts can make them look confusing. 1) /g/ vs /ɡ/ - In IPA, the correct symbol for the “g” sound (as in “go”) is usually the “script g”: ɡ (Unicode U+0261). - Many fonts display it as a normal “g” that looks like the printed single-storey form, while your usual text “g” may be the double-storey form (g). - Some dictionaries and websites use plain “g” because it’s easier to type, even though strict IPA prefers ɡ. So often /g/ and /ɡ/ are intended to mean the same sound in dictionary contexts. 2) /r/ vs /ɾ/ vs /ɹ/ These are genuinely different IPA symbols: - /r/ = alveolar trill (like a rolled r in languages that use it) - /ɾ/ = alveolar tap (single contact) - /ɹ/ = alveolar approximant (common English “r”) But dictionaries may use /r/ to represent the approximant /ɹ/ for convenience. That’s why you can’t always assume strict IPA values in dictionary phonemic transcriptions. Practical reading strategy: - In broad dictionary transcription, interpret /r/ as “the language’s r phoneme” (often [ɹ] in English). - In narrow phonetic transcription, interpret symbols strictly: [r] trill, [ɾ] tap, [ɹ] approximant. If you want a quick “spotter’s guide” to common IPA characters that get confused due to fonts (like /ɑ/ vs /a/; /ɡ/ vs /g/; /ʊ/ vs /u/), I can list them.
Yes, please. Those confuse me all the time.

Here’s a “commonly confused IPA characters” spotter’s guide, focusing on what you’re likely to see in dictionaries and phonetics resources: 1) ɡ (IPA) vs g (regular Latin) - ɡ = IPA “script g” (voiced velar stop) - g = may be used informally to mean the same thing Tip: if you see either in an English dictionary transcription, it’s almost always the “g” in “go.” 2) ɑ vs a - ɑ = open back unrounded vowel (“palm” in many systems) - a = open front unrounded vowel (cardinal [a]) or used in some systems for the “trap” or “palm” area depending on language Tip: English dictionaries often use ɑː (or ɑ) for “palm,” while a is more common in transcriptions for other languages or broader IPA usage. Don’t assume they’re interchangeable. 3) ɒ vs ɔ vs o - ɒ = open back rounded (often “lot” in British-oriented dictionaries) - ɔ = open-mid back rounded (often “thought” vowel quality region) - o = close-mid back rounded (often the “pure o” vowel in many languages; English “goat” is usually a diphthong /oʊ/ or /əʊ/, not a pure [o]) Tip: pay attention to the openness: ɒ is more open than ɔ, and o is more closed than ɔ. 4) u vs ʊ - u = close back rounded (often [u]) - ʊ = near-close near-back rounded (“foot”) Tip: ʊ has the “hook” shape; it’s not just a stylistic u. 5) e vs ɛ - e = close-mid front unrounded - ɛ = open-mid front unrounded (“dress” in many English dictionary systems) Tip: some dictionaries use /e/ for “dress,” others use /ɛ/. Learn the convention of your source. 6) ɜ vs ə - ə = schwa (mid central, typically unstressed) - ɜ = open-mid central (often “nurse” quality base; with length /ɜː/ in some systems) Tip: if there’s ː, it’s likely the “nurse” vowel category; schwa is usually short and unstressed. 7) ʃ vs ʂ (less common in English dictionaries) - ʃ = “sh” - ʂ = retroflex fricative (used in some languages) Tip: in English contexts you almost always want ʃ. 8) j vs ʝ vs ɟ (other languages) - j in IPA = “y” sound (palatal approximant), not “j” as in “jam” Tip: this one is huge—IPA /j/ corresponds to “y” in English spelling. 9) ʔ vs ʕ (don’t mix) - ʔ = glottal stop - ʕ = voiced pharyngeal fricative/approximant (Semitic languages, etc.) Tip: in English phonetics, ʔ is the one you’ll see. If you tell me which symbols you most often mistake for each other, I can tailor the list and give a “how to visually recognize them” tip for each.
The /j/ thing is big. I keep reading /j/ like “j” in normal spelling. Can you explain /j/ and /dʒ/ clearly?

Definitely. IPA /j/: - /j/ is the palatal approximant. - In normal English spelling, it’s the “y” sound at the start of “yes.” - It’s not a fricative; it’s a glide/approximant, meaning the tongue approaches the palate but doesn’t create strong friction. So: - “yes” = /jes/ (in many transcriptions) - “yesterday” might start /ˈjest.../ When you see /j/ in IPA, think “y,” not “j.” IPA /dʒ/: - /dʒ/ is an affricate: a stop [d] released into a fricative [ʒ]. - In normal English spelling, it’s the “j” sound in “jam,” and also the sound often spelled “g” before e/i/y (in many words). - It’s written as two symbols because it has two parts (stop + fricative), but it functions like one consonant in the syllable. So: - “jam” = /dʒæm/ - “measure” has /ʒ/ alone: /ˈmeʒə/ (no [d] stop part) Quick contrast: - /j/ is like a quick “y” glide: /j/ + vowel, as in /juː/ (“you”). - /dʒ/ has that stop onset: /dʒ/ + vowel, as in /dʒuː/ (“Jew” in some transcriptions) — note: the point is the consonant difference. Reading tip: - If you see /j/ before /uː/ (like /juː/), that’s “yoo.” - If you see /dʒ/ before a vowel, that’s “j” as in “job.” Want a few practice items where /j/ appears in the middle (like /ˈedjʊkeɪt/) so it stops feeling weird?
Yes, give me several words with /j/ in different positions and show the IPA. And explain any tricky ones.

Here are examples with /j/ in different positions. I’ll keep them fairly common and explain any that are tricky to read. 1) /jes/ - /j/ + /e/ + /s/ → “yes” 2) /jɛt/ - “yet” (in a system using /ɛ/ for “dress”) 3) /juː/ - “you”: /j/ + /uː/ (“yoo”) 4) /ˈjɒŋ/ or /jɒŋ/ - “yong” isn’t a common everyday English word, so instead let’s do a more standard one: 4) /ˈjɪə/ - “year” in a non-rhotic style: the /ɪə/ indicates a glide toward schwa; /j/ starts it: “y-” + “ear” 5) /ˈvɑːljuː/ or /ˈvæljuː/ (dictionary-dependent) - “value” - This one is tricky because many speakers have a /j/ (“y”) after /l/ before /uː/: /ˈvæljuː/ (“val-yoo”). - Some transcriptions may simplify or vary; but when you see /lj/ or /ljuː/, it means the /l/ is followed by a palatal glide /j/ into /uː/. 6) /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ - “educate” (your example) - The /dj/ sequence means /d/ followed by /j/ glide, then /ʊ/. It’s not /dʒ/; it’s [d] + [j] (though in fast speech it may sound affricate-like, which is why it confuses learners). 7) /ˈmɪljən/ - “million” - /lj/ cluster: /l/ + /j/ (“ly” sound). Then schwa-like /ə/ in the last syllable. 8) /ˈpɒpjʊlə/ (dictionary-dependent) - “popular” - /pj/ = /p/ + /j/ glide: “pyu-” type sequence. Reading tips for clusters: - /CjV/ (like /pjʊ/, /tjʊ/, /kjʊ/) means consonant + “y” glide + vowel. - Don’t turn /dj/ into /dʒ/ automatically. /dʒ/ is specifically the affricate; /dj/ is a cluster. If you want, we can do a mini-drill: I’ll give you 10 IPA forms containing /j/, and you tell me whether you think it’s “y” glide /j/ or the affricate /dʒ/—that’s a common reading hurdle.
Yes, do that drill.

Here are 10 items. For each, answer: does it contain the glide /j/ (“y” sound), or the affricate /dʒ/ (“j” sound), or both? 1) /juː/ 2) /dʒuːs/ 3) /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ 4) /ˈdʒʌdʒ/ 5) /ˈmeʒə/ 6) /ˈjɛləʊ/ 7) /ˈsɪndʒə/ 8) /ˈstjuːdənt/ 9) /ˈdʒɒb/ 10) /ˈkjʊəriəs/
1 glide, 2 affricate, 3 glide, 4 affricate, 5 neither?, 6 glide, 7 affricate?, 8 glide, 9 affricate, 10 glide.

Good. Let’s check each: 1) /juː/ = glide /j/ (correct) 2) /dʒuːs/ = affricate /dʒ/ (correct) (“juice”) 3) /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ = glide /j/ in /dj/ (correct) 4) /ˈdʒʌdʒ/ = affricate /dʒ/ (correct) (“judge”) 5) /ˈmeʒə/ = neither /j/ nor /dʒ/ (correct) It has /ʒ/ (voiced “zh”), which is related to the second part of /dʒ/ but without the initial [d]. 6) /ˈjɛləʊ/ = glide /j/ (correct) (“yellow”) 7) /ˈsɪndʒə/ = it contains /dʒ/ (affricate) if that’s how it’s segmented (often “singer” isn’t this; but as a sound sequence, /ndʒ/ is n + dʒ). So your “affricate?” is correct. 8) /ˈstjuːdənt/ = glide /j/ (correct) That /tj/ sequence is consonant + /j/ glide. 9) /ˈdʒɒb/ = affricate /dʒ/ (correct) (“job”) 10) /ˈkjʊəriəs/ = glide /j/ (correct) /kjʊə/ is k + y + vowel sequence. So you’re reading /j/ correctly. Do you want to tackle another frequent confusion: /ŋ/ vs /nɡ/ (like “sing” /sɪŋ/), or maybe vowel length and the colon /ː/ and when it matters?
/ŋ/ vs /nɡ/ please. I see /ŋ/ and I don’t know whether to pronounce a g.

/ŋ/ is the velar nasal: the “ng” sound at the end of “sing.” It’s a single consonant, not necessarily /n/ + /g/. How it’s articulated: - Like /n/, it’s nasal (air goes through the nose). - But the tongue closure is at the velum (soft palate), like /k/ or /g/, not at the alveolar ridge like /n/. Do you pronounce a [g]? - If the transcription is /ŋ/ alone (e.g., /sɪŋ/), that indicates no separate /g/ sound segment—just the nasal. - If the transcription is /ŋɡ/ (e.g., /fɪŋɡə/ for “finger” in some transcriptions), that indicates the nasal followed by a /g/ segment. So the answer is: follow what’s written. - /ŋ/ = just the nasal “ng” - /ŋɡ/ = “ngg” sequence (nasal + g) Why spelling misleads: - English spelling “ng” can represent /ŋ/ (sing) or /ŋɡ/ (finger) or /n/ + /dʒ/ in some cases (rare, like “angel” is /ˈeɪndʒəl/ with /ndʒ/). Quick examples: - “sing” /sɪŋ/ - “singing” /ˈsɪŋɪŋ/ - “finger” often /ˈfɪŋɡə/ (in non-rhotic style) or /ˈfɪŋɡɚ/ (rhotic), showing /ŋɡ/ Reading tip: if you see /ŋ/ at the end of a syllable and there’s no /ɡ/ after it, don’t add a /g/. If you want, give me 5 words you’ve seen with “ng” and their IPA from your dictionary; we can confirm whether it’s /ŋ/ or /ŋɡ/ in each.
I saw “sing” /sɪŋ/, “singer” /ˈsɪŋə/, “finger” /ˈfɪŋɡə/, “longer” /ˈlɒŋɡə/, and “language” /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/. Why is “singer” not /ŋɡ/ then?

Your dictionary is showing a common pattern: - “sing” /sɪŋ/ → /ŋ/ only - “singer” /ˈsɪŋə/ → still /ŋ/ only - “finger” /ˈfɪŋɡə/ → /ŋɡ/ - “longer” /ˈlɒŋɡə/ → /ŋɡ/ - “language” /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/ → /ŋɡ/ plus /w/ etc. Why “singer” lacks /g/: In many words where “ng” is part of a morpheme ending (like “sing” + “-er”), the /g/ isn’t present as a separate consonant in the modern pronunciation, so the derived form keeps /ŋ/ without adding /g/: /sɪŋ/ → /ˈsɪŋə/. Why “finger/longer/language” have /ŋɡ/: In those words, historically and phonologically the /g/ is part of the underlying cluster /ŋɡ/ in many speakers’ systems, so dictionaries often represent it as /ŋɡ/. Reading takeaway: - Don’t assume “ng” spelling means /ŋɡ/. The dictionary’s IPA tells you whether there is a /ɡ/ segment. - If you see /ŋə/ as in /ˈsɪŋə/, pronounce it as “sing-uh” without a hard /g/ segment. Also note “language” /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/: - /dʒ/ at the end is the affricate “j” sound. - /w/ is the “w” glide. So it’s a nice example of multiple IPA “chunks” in one word. Do you want to practice reading a longer IPA transcription fluently—like taking a full word and converting it to a pronounceable guide without losing the IPA accuracy?
Yes. Take “international phonetics” maybe. Show me how to go from /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl fəˈnɛtɪks/ to a clear pronunciation plan.

Let’s do it systematically: IPA → stress pattern → syllable plan → sound plan. Phrase: /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl fəˈnɛtɪks/ 1) Identify stressed syllables - “international” /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ - secondary stress: ˌ before /ɪn/ - primary stress: ˈ before /næ/ - “phonetics” /fəˈnɛtɪks/ - primary stress: ˈ before /nɛ/ So the main beats are: NASH and NET, with a smaller beat on IN. 2) Mark syllable nuclei (vowels) to segment international: - /ɪ/ in /ɪn/ → syllable 1 - /ə/ in /tə/ → syllable 2 - /æ/ in /næ/ → syllable 3 - /ə/ in /ʃə/ → syllable 4 - /ə/ in /nəl/ → syllable 5 phonetics: - /ə/ in /fə/ → syllable 1 - /ɛ/ in /nɛ/ → syllable 2 - /ɪ/ in /tɪks/ → syllable 3 3) Convert each syllable to a “sound plan” international: - /ˌɪn/ = “in” (secondary stress: say it clearly but not as the main beat) - /tə/ = “tuh” (schwa, light) - /ˈnæ/ = “na” with /æ/ (primary stress: open, clear vowel) - /ʃə/ = “shuh” (light) - /nəl/ = “nuhl” (light; could be [nəl] or [nɫ̩] in narrow forms) phonetics: - /fə/ = “fuh” (light schwa) - /ˈnɛ/ = “neh” with /ɛ/ (primary stress) - /tɪks/ = “tiks” (light) 4) Put it together with rhythm IN-tuh-NASH-uh-nuhl fuh-NET-iks 5) What to prioritize - Get the stressed vowels right: /æ/ in /næ/ and /ɛ/ in /nɛ/ - Keep schwas reduced: /tə/, /ʃə/, /nəl/, /fə/ - Keep consonants crisp: /ʃ/ in -nash-, /t/ in -tiks If you want, we can do the same with a trickier phrase with more consonant clusters, and I’ll show how to avoid “inserting extra vowels” when reading IPA.
Yes, clusters are hard. I see /str/ /spl/ /θr/ and I don’t know how to pronounce without adding a vowel.

Consonant clusters are basically sequences of consonants within one syllable onset or coda. IPA helps because it shows you exactly which consonants are present—no extra vowel unless a vowel symbol appears. How to handle them without inserting vowels: 1) Start from the last consonant of the cluster and build backward. 2) Keep airflow continuous if possible. 3) Use a slow-motion approach: hold the first consonant longer, then transition. Let’s do your examples: A) /str/ (as in /striːt/ “street”) - /s/ then /t/ then /r/ Method: - Start with /t/ + /r/: say /tr/ as one unit (like “tree” starts). - Then add /s/ at the front: /s/ + /tr/ → /str/. Key: /s/ is a fricative; you can start it and then move into /t/ closure. B) /spl/ (as in /spliːt/ “split”) - Build /pl/ first (like “play” starts). - Then add /s/ in front: /spl/. C) /θr/ (as in /θriː/ “three”) - /θ/ is a fricative made with the tongue tip near the teeth. - /r/ is an approximant. Method: hold /θ/ (continuous air) and then move tongue shape into /r/ without adding a vowel. IPA reading tip: - If you see /s t r/ in a row with no vowel between them, do not insert a vowel. - If you see a syllable boundary dot, it can help you see whether the cluster is within one syllable or split. Mini practice list (all one-syllable onsets): 1) /striːt/ (street) 2) /skwiːz/ (squeeze) (/skw/) 3) /splæʃ/ (splash) 4) /θriː/ (three) 5) /sprɪŋ/ (spring) Try reading those slowly, focusing on keeping them as one syllable each.
I can read /striːt/ and /θriː/ but /skwiːz/ confuses me. Is /kw/ one sound? And what is /w/ exactly?

/kw/ is not one sound in IPA; it’s a cluster: /k/ followed by /w/. What /w/ is: - /w/ is a voiced labial-velar approximant. - “Labial” = involves the lips (rounding/closing). - “Velar” = the back of the tongue approaches the velum. - “Approximant” = close but not enough to create friction like a fricative. It’s the consonant at the start of “we” /wiː/ and “water” /ˈwɔːtə/ (in a system with /ɔː/). How to pronounce /kw/: - Start with /k/ closure (back of tongue against velum). - Release /k/ directly into the /w/ shape: lips rounded and tongue back-ish. - Then go into the vowel /iː/. So /skwiːz/: - /s/ (hissing) → /k/ (closure) → /w/ (rounded glide) → /iː/ (long “ee”) → /z/ (buzzing) It’s “squeeze.” Reading tip: /w/ often looks like it might be a vowel marker because of the lip rounding, but in IPA it’s a consonant (a glide). If you want, we can contrast /w/ with /v/ and /uː/ since those are frequently mixed up in perception: - /w/ is a glide consonant. - /uː/ is a vowel. - /v/ is a fricative with lip-teeth contact.
Yes, /w/ vs /uː/ especially. They feel similar.

They’re related in the sense that both involve lip rounding and a “back” tongue posture, but they function differently. 1) /uː/ (vowel) - You can hold it steadily: [uːːː] - Tongue high and back; lips rounded; continuous voicing - It’s the nucleus of a syllable (it can carry stress) 2) /w/ (consonant glide) - You typically can’t hold it as a stable vowel nucleus in normal English phonotactics; it’s a transition into a vowel. - It’s very brief and “leads into” the following vowel. - Articulatorily, it’s like moving quickly from a [u]-like posture into the next vowel. A practical test: - Say /uː/ and hold it: “ooooo” (steady). - Say /wiː/ (“we”): the /w/ is the initial glide; you quickly move into /iː/. If you try to hold the /w/ like a vowel, you’ll tend to turn it into [u] or [ʊ], which changes the syllable structure. In IPA reading: - /uː/ will appear in the vowel slot (between consonants or as the syllable nucleus). - /w/ will appear in consonant positions, typically before a vowel. Example contrast: - /wiː/ (“we”) begins with /w/ then /iː/ - /uː/ (“oo” sound) is just the vowel; no /w/ at the start. If you want practice reading, I can give pairs like /wiː/ vs /uːiː/ (the second is unusual in English but shows the difference). Or more realistic: words where spelling has “w” but IPA shows /w/, versus words where spelling has “oo” and IPA shows /uː/.
I think I get it. Now, how do I type IPA? I can’t easily type /ʃ/ /ə/ /ɜː/ etc.

Practical options, from easiest to more advanced: 1) Copy-paste - If you’re using online dictionaries, you can often copy the IPA directly. - For study notes, copy the transcription and paste it into your document. 2) Use an IPA keyboard (web-based) - Search for “IPA keyboard” websites: they let you click symbols like ʃ ə ɜː and copy them. - This is the most beginner-friendly way. 3) Install an IPA keyboard layout - Some operating systems let you add an “IPA” or “Unicode” keyboard. - Alternatively, you can use a “Compose key” setup or custom layout to type special characters. 4) Use Unicode input methods - On some systems, you can type Unicode codes (like U+0259 for ə). This is powerful but not beginner-friendly. 5) Use X-SAMPA or ASCII approximations - In some contexts, people use ASCII-based phonetic notation (like “@” for schwa). - But if your goal is reading dictionaries, it’s better to learn the actual IPA symbols. If you tell me what device/OS you’re on (Windows/macOS/Linux/iOS/Android), I can give a straightforward method tailored to it.
I use a laptop. But let’s not get too into OS details. Can you just show me a simple ASCII approximation I can use in my notes, and how it maps to IPA?

Sure. The most common ASCII-based system is X-SAMPA. Here’s a small mapping for the symbols you’ve been dealing with: Common consonants: - ʃ = S - ʒ = Z - θ = T - ð = D - ŋ = N - tʃ = tS - dʒ = dZ - j = j (same character, but remember it means “y” sound in IPA) Common vowels: - ə = @ - ɜː = 3: (or sometimes 3:) - ɪ = I - iː = i: - ʊ = U - uː = u: - æ = { - ɒ = Q (often used for “lot” vowel in British-oriented sets) - ɔː = O: - ʌ = V - ɑː = A: Stress/length: - ˈ (primary stress) = ' - ˌ (secondary stress) = , (comma in many X-SAMPA conventions) - ː (length) = : Examples: - /fəˈnɛtɪks/ could be written roughly as f@'nEtIks (using E for ɛ) - /ˌɪntəˈnæʃənəl/ → ,Int@'n{S@n@l Caution: - ASCII systems vary a bit, and not everyone uses exactly the same mapping. But for personal notes, a consistent mapping is what matters. If you want, we can convert a few of your earlier IPA words into this ASCII notation so you can type them quickly.
Convert these: /ˈedjʊkeɪt/, /ˈmeʒə/, /ˈpɜːpəl/, /kəˌmjuːnɪˈkeɪʃən/.

Here are reasonable X-SAMPA-style approximations: 1) /ˈedjʊkeɪt/ - IPA: /ˈe d j ʊ k eɪ t/ - ASCII: 'edjUkeIt Notes: /eɪ/ → eI, /ʊ/ → U 2) /ˈmeʒə/ - ASCII: 'meZ@ Notes: /ʒ/ → Z, /ə/ → @ 3) /ˈpɜːpəl/ - ASCII: 'p3:p@l Notes: /ɜː/ → 3: ; /ə/ → @ 4) /kəˌmjuːnɪˈkeɪʃən/ - ASCII: k@,mju:nI'keIS@n Notes: /uː/ → u: ; /ɪ/ → I ; /eɪ/ → eI ; /ʃ/ → S ; /ə/ → @ If you keep these mappings handy, you can type fast and still preserve the phonetic distinctions. Nice—most are correct. Let’s go through each with explanations, especially where the IPA gives you clues. 1) /ˈkæmərə/ = “camera” - Stress: ˈ on first syllable: CAM- - /ə/ in the last syllable shows reduction. 2) /dəˈmɑːkrəsi/ = “democracy” - Stress on /mɑː/: de-MOC-ra-cy (in this transcription) - /d ə/ at start: reduced vowel. 3) /ˈtʃɒklət/ = “chocolate” - /tʃ/ = “ch” - /ɒ/ “lot” vowel - Stress on first syllable. - /-lət/ ends with schwa-like vowel (here written /ə/). 4) /ɪkˈspɪəriəns/ = “experience” - Stress on /spɪə/ (the syllable after ˈ) - /ɪk-/ is a reduced-ish prefix here, unstressed. - /ɪə/ indicates that “ear” type vowel sequence in non-rhotic style. 5) /ˈmɪnɪt/ = “minute” (the time unit) - Stress on first syllable. - Both vowels are /ɪ/ in this transcription. 6) /əˈbaʊt/ = “about” - Stress on second syllable: a-BOUT. - First vowel is schwa /ə/ (unstressed). 7) /ˈkʌlʧə/ or /ˈkʌltʃə/ = “culture” - Your answer “culture” is correct. - The IPA shows either /lʧ/ as a single symbol /ʧ/ in some transcription traditions, or explicitly /ltʃ/. - /ə/ at end shows reduced final syllable. 8) /ˌriːəˈlæks/ = “re-relax” or “relax” with a prefix “re-” depending on context - Your uncertainty makes sense: this transcription suggests /riːə-/ then primary stress on /læks/. - It could correspond to something like “re-relax” (doing it again), or “re-” + “relax” with a syllable boundary. - The key reading point: ˌ indicates secondary stress on /riː/, and ˈ indicates primary stress on /læks/. - /riːə/ indicates two vowel nuclei /iː/ then /ə/, so it’s two syllables at the start: ree-uh-. 9) /ˈdɪfɪkəlt/ = “difficult” - First vowel schwa-like /ə/ (written /ə/ here as part of /kən/) - /əʊ/ is the “goat” diphthong. 12) /ˌɪnfəˈmeɪʃən/ = “information” - Secondary stress on /ɪn/ - Primary stress on /meɪ/ - /ʃən/ ending So you’re reading stress and vowels well. Excellent—these are all essentially correct. Let’s confirm and use them to reinforce the symbols you requested. 1) /ˈwɜːkə/ “worker” - /ɜː/ = “nurse” vowel, long. - Final /ə/ schwa. - Stress: first syllable. 2) /ˈwɜːld/ “world” - /ɜː/ again. - /ld/ cluster at end. 3) /ˈʃɜːt/ “shirt” - /ʃ/ = “sh” - /ɜː/ = “nurse” - Stress: first syllable. 4) /əˈlɔː/ “al(l)aw/allow” depending on word list - As a standalone, /əˈlɔː/ matches “a law” as a phrase if spaced, but as a single word it could represent “allure” in some systems (though that usually has more segments) or “allow” in certain transcriptions would be /əˈlaʊ/. This item demonstrates the reading: /ə/ unstressed, /ɔː/ long vowel. 5) /ˈbɔːdər/ “border” - /ɔː/ long “thought” vowel. - /ər/ indicates an r-colored or /ə/+/r/ sequence depending on transcription style. Some dictionaries would write /ˈbɔːdə/ (non-rhotic). - Stress: first syllable. 6) /ˈfʊtprɪnt/ “footprint” - /ʊ/ in “foot” - cluster /tpr/ across the morpheme boundary—no extra vowel written, so don’t insert one. 7) /ˈgʊdnəs/ “goodness” - /ʊ/ again, and /ə/ in the last syllable. 8) /ˈlɒŋɡə/ “longer” - /ɒ/ “lot” - /ŋɡ/ shows “ng” plus a pronounced /g/ segment. - Final /ə/ schwa. - Stress: first syllable. 9) /ˈlæŋɡwɪdʒ/ “language” - /æ/ “trap” - /ŋɡw/ cluster: /ŋ/ + /ɡ/ + /w/ - /dʒ/ affricate at end. 10) /ˈmeʒə/ “measure” - /ʒ/ voiced “zh” - final schwa. 11) /ˈpleʒə/ “pleasure” - also /ʒ/ and schwa. - Stress first syllable. 12) /ɪnˈdʒɔɪ/ “enjoy” - stress on second syllable: -JOY - /dʒ/ affricate. 13) /əˈreɪndʒmənt/ “arrangement” - schwa /ə/ first - /eɪ/ diphthong - /ndʒ/ cluster includes /dʒ/ affricate - /mənt/ ends with schwa-like /ə/ in /mə/ plus /nt/ cluster 14) /ˌɔːɡənaɪˈzeɪʃən/ “organisation/organization” - secondary stress on /ɔː/ - primary stress on /zeɪ/ - /ʃən/ ending - /ɡ/ here may appear as ɡ in strict IPA The only ambiguous one is #4 because without spelling context it could map to different items, but as a reading exercise it did its job: /ə/ + stressed /ɔː/ syllable. If you want, we can do a final “skills summary”: a compact set of rules you can apply whenever you see an IPA transcription in a dictionary, so you don’t have to relearn each time.
Yes, give me that compact set of rules. And then maybe a plan for how to study IPA for 10 minutes a day.

Compact rules for reading dictionary IPA (practical): 1) Start with stress marks - Find ˈ (primary stress). The syllable right after it is the main beat. - If you see ˌ, that syllable gets a smaller beat. 2) Identify vowels first - Each vowel symbol (or diphthong) is usually the core of a syllable. - /ə/ is a big clue: that syllable is weak/unstressed and reduced. 3) Treat common multi-symbol consonants as units - /tʃ/ = “ch” - /dʒ/ = “j” - /θ/ = “th” (thin) - /ð/ = “th” (this) - /ʃ/ = “sh” - /ʒ/ = “zh” - /ŋ/ = “ng” (single consonant) 4) Read length marks as “hold it” - /ː/ means the vowel is long (and often slightly different in quality too). Examples: /ɔː ɜː ɑː iː uː/. 5) Diphthongs are glides, not two separate vowels - /aɪ, aʊ, eɪ, ɔɪ, əʊ/ etc. = one syllable nucleus with movement. 6) Don’t add sounds that aren’t written - If there’s no vowel symbol between consonants, it’s a consonant cluster. - If it’s /ŋ/ and not /ŋɡ/, don’t add a /g/. 7) Know your source’s conventions - Some dictionaries use /r/ as a simplified symbol (meaning [ɹ]). - Some omit /r/ in certain positions (non-rhotic style). - Some use /i/ for the final “happy” vowel instead of /iː/. 10 minutes a day IPA study plan (simple and sustainable): Minute 1–2: Symbol review - Pick 3–5 symbols you keep forgetting (e.g., /ə ɜː ɒ ɔː ʊ ʒ ŋ/). - Look at them and say their keyword examples out loud. Minute 3–6: Read 5 dictionary entries - Choose 5 words you encountered that day. - Copy their IPA. - Mark stress (underline the stressed syllable). - Say them slowly, then at normal speed. Minute 7–9: Minimal contrast practice - Pick one contrast: /iː/ vs /ɪ/, /uː/ vs /ʊ/, /s/ vs /z/, /ʃ/ vs /ʒ/. - Say 5 pairs (or read them from a list) and focus on one difference. Minute 10: Quick self-check - Record yourself saying 2 of the words. - Compare with the dictionary audio if available, or just listen for whether stress and vowel qualities match your intention. If you tell me the exact set of IPA symbols your dictionary uses for vowels (for example whether it uses /e/ or /ɛ/, /ɒ/ or /ɑ/), I can tailor a one-page “learn these 15 symbols first” list so your practice is focused.
